inline_review:
commit cd78854e55ee90102333f0331c58417c16e8d798
Author: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

mm/damon: support MADV_COLLAPSE via DAMOS_COLLAPSE scheme action

This commit introduces the DAMOS_COLLAPSE action to DAMON. It allows DAMON
to synchronously collapse memory regions into huge pages by leveraging the
existing MADV_COLLAPSE mechanism.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/damon/20260313000816.79933-1-sj@kernel.org/

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/mm/damon/vaddr.c b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
> index b069dbc7e3d2..dd5f2d7027ac 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/vaddr.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/vaddr.c
> @@ -903,6 +903,9 @@ static unsigned long damon_va_apply_scheme(struct damon_ctx *ctx,
>  	case DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE:
>  		madv_action = MADV_NOHUGEPAGE;
>  		break;
> +	case DAMOS_COLLAPSE:
> +		madv_action = MADV_COLLAPSE;

Could exposing MADV_COLLAPSE to automated continuous execution via DAMOS
trigger a pre-existing use-after-free in the core madvise logic?

In madvise_collapse(), the lock_dropped boolean tracks whether the lock is
currently dropped and tells madvise_walk_vmas() if the lock was ever dropped.

If collapse_single_pmd() drops the mmap lock, *lock_dropped is set to true.
On the next loop iteration, madvise_collapse() re-acquires the lock,
validates the VMA, and resets *lock_dropped = false to avoid double-locking.
If a subsequent iteration does not drop the lock, the function returns to
madvise_walk_vmas() with lock_dropped set to false.

Because madvise_walk_vmas() sees false, it assumes the lock was continuously
held and the original VMA pointer is still safe. However, another thread
could have freed the original VMA while the lock was temporarily dropped,
leading to a use-after-free when madvise_walk_vmas() dereferences
vma->vm_end.

While this flaw pre-dates this patch, does introducing DAMOS_COLLAPSE expose
it to continuous background execution by kdamond, making the race condition
much more likely to occur in practice?

> +		break;
>  	case DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT:
>  	case DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD:

Target Commit:
commit cd78854e55ee90102333f0331c58417c16e8d798
Author: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 30 14:57:58 2026 +0000

    mm/damon: support MADV_COLLAPSE via DAMOS_COLLAPSE scheme action
    
    This patch set introces a new action:  DAMOS_COLLAPSE.
    
    For DAMOS_HUGEPAGE and DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE to work, khugepaged should be
    working, since it relies on hugepage_madvise to add a new slot. This
    slot should be picked up by khugepaged and eventually collapse (or
    not, if we are using DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE) the pages. If THP is not
    enabled, khugepaged will not be working, and therefore no collapse
    will happen.
    
    DAMOS_COLLAPSE eventually calls madvise_collapse, which will collapse
    the address range synchronously.
    
    This new action may be required to support autotuning with hugepage
    as a goal[1].
    
    [1]: https://lore.kernel.org/damon/20260313000816.79933-1-sj@kernel.org/
    
    ---------
    Benchmarks:
    
    Tests were performed in an ARM physical server with MariaDB 10.5 and
    sysbench. Read only benchmark was perform with uniform row hitting,
    which means that all rows will be access with equal probability.
    
    T n, D h: THP set to never, DAMON action set to hugepage
    T m, D h: THP set to madvise, DAMON action set to hugepage
    T n, D c: THP set to never, DAMON action set to collapse
    
    Memory consumption. Lower is better.
    
    +------------------+----------+----------+----------+
    |                  | T n, D h | T m, D h | T n, D c |
    +------------------+----------+----------+----------+
    | Total memory use | 2.07     | 2.09     | 2.07     |
    | Huge pages       | 0        | 1.3      | 1.25     |
    +------------------+----------+----------+----------+
    
    Performance in TPS (Transactions Per Second). Higher is better.
    
    T n, D h: 18324.57
    T n, D h 18452.69
    T n, D c: 18432.17
    
    Performance counter
    
    I got the number of L1 D/I TLB accesses and the number a D/I TLB
    accesses that triggered a page walk. I divided the second by the
    first to get the percentage of page walkes per TLB access. The
    lower the better.
    
    +---------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+
    |               | T n, D h     | T m, D h     | T n, D c     |
    +---------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+
    | L1 DTLB       | 127248242753 | 125431020479 | 125327001821 |
    | L1 ITLB       | 80332558619  | 79346759071  | 79298139590  |
    | DTLB walk     | 75011087     | 52800418     | 55895794     |
    | ITLB walk     | 71577076     | 71505137     | 67262140     |
    | DTLB % misses | 0.058948623  | 0.042095183  | 0.044599961  |
    | ITLB % misses | 0.089100954  | 0.090117275  | 0.084821839  |
    +---------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+
    
    - We can see that DAMOS "hugepage" action works only when THP is set
      to madvise. "collapse" action works even when THP is set to never.
    - Performance for "collapse" action is slightly lower than "hugepage"
      action and THP madvise.
    - Memory consumption is slighly lower for "collapse" than "hugepage"
      with THP madvise. This is due to the khugepage collapses all VMAs,
      while "collapse" action only collapses the VMAs in the hot region.
    - There is an improvement in THP utilization when collapse through
      "hugepage" or "collapse" actions are triggered.
    - "collapse" action is performance synchronously, which means that
      page collapses happen earlier and more rapidly. This can be
      useful or not, depending on the scenario.
    
    Collapse action just adds a new option to chose the correct system
    balance.
    
    Changes
    ---------
    RFC v2 -> v1:
    Fixed a missing comma in the selftest python stript
    Added performance benchmarks
    
    RFC v1 -> RFC v2:
    Added benchmarks
    Added damos_filter_type documentation for new action to fix kernel-doc
    
    Signed-off-by: Asier Gutierrez <gutierrez.asier@huawei-partners.com>

  - ``lru_deprio``: Deprioritize the region on its LRU lists.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Safety of `do_madvise()` in DAMON Context**

Okay, so I'm digging into `damos_madvise` and how it uses `do_madvise`. The initial thought is to make sure I haven't missed any other places in the code where this happens.  `do_madvise` itself checks `madvise_should_skip()`, which is good. Let's see... inside `damos_madvise`, it gets a `mm_struct`, calls `do_madvise` with that `mm`, and then handles the return, which indicates success/failure. A failure here is indicated by `do_madvise` returning a negative error code but `damos_madvise` returns `0`.

The key question is: Is it safe to call `do_madvise()` on an arbitrary `mm` (a memory map that doesn't belong to the current process), without explicit checks within `do_madvise` ensuring `current->mm == mm`?  `process_madvise_remote_valid()` checks `behavior` validity but `process_madvise` indirectly calls `do_madvise()` itself. The crucial difference is that `damos_madvise()` calls `do_madvise(mm, ...)` directly!

Let's break down `do_madvise()` step by step:
```c
	struct madvise_behavior madv_behavior = {
		.mm = mm,
		.behavior = behavior,
		.tlb = &tlb,
	};
	error = madvise_lock(&madv_behavior);
	if (error)
		return error;
	madvise_init_tlb(&madv_behavior);
	error = madvise_do_behavior(start, len_in, &madv_behavior);
	madvise_finish_tlb(&madv_behavior);
	madvise_unlock(&madv_behavior);
```

Wait a second, `do_madvise()` is normally called through the `madvise` system call:
```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE3(madvise, ...)
{
	return do_madvise(current->mm, start, len_in, behavior);
}
```
Does it assume that `madv_behavior.mm` is always `current->mm` somewhere?

Okay, let's check `madvise_do_behavior()`. It looks like there's a reference to the `mm` in `get_untagged_addr()`:
```c
static int madvise_do_behavior(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,
		struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)
{
...
	if (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior)) {
...
	}

	range->start = get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start);
...
```

Let's check `get_untagged_addr()`:
```c
static inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,
		unsigned long start)
{
	return current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :
				   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);
}
```
Aha! `get_untagged_addr()` *does* handle the case where `current->mm != mm`. That's good.

Wait, there's another check in `madvise_cold_or_pageout_pte_range()` that calls `fatal_signal_pending(current)`, which is fine. It refers to the DAMON thread.

Okay, is `madvise_collapse` safe for a remote `mm`? `madvise_collapse()` doesn't seem to use `current->mm`, it gets the `mm` from the `vma->vm_mm`.

Wait, in `madvise_collapse`, there's:
```c
		result = collapse_single_pmd(addr, vma, lock_dropped, cc);
```
Are there any `current` dependencies inside `collapse_single_pmd` or `hugepage_vma_revalidate`? Time to dig into those functions.

    "thought": "**DAMON and `do_madvise()`: A Deep Dive into Locking and Remote Operations**

Okay, so I'm investigating how DAMON interacts with `do_madvise()`. Initially, it seemed like there wasn't a direct dependency on `current`, but I need to be careful since `do_madvise()` is used within the DAMON thread, which is a kernel thread and its behavior might be different in this context.

My main concern is around locking, especially with `mmap_write_lock` or `mmap_read_lock`. I see `madvise_collapse()` explicitly expects `mmap_read_lock`, and might even drop it, which is the key thing I am interested in.

Looking into `DAMON_COLLAPSE`, there doesn't seem to be anything unusual about how `damos_madvise` is used. If `do_madvise()` fails, `damos_madvise()` returns 0, but I don't see any immediate locking issues in the function.

I see that `do_madvise()` itself handles the `mmap` locks, which is good. That confirms that `damos_madvise` runs outside any `mmap_read_lock()`. I know this is because `damon_va_apply_scheme` calls `damos_madvise()` directly, which in turn calls `do_madvise()` without holding `mmap_read_lock(mm)`.

However, `mmap_read_lock` *is* acquired in other parts of `damon_va_apply_scheme` like `damos_va_stat` and `damos_va_migrate`. So that's important to keep in mind.

It's clear that `damos_madvise()` itself doesn't directly acquire `mmap_read_lock(mm)`. This is because `do_madvise()` does. That's fine for us and exactly as intended.

If we're using `MADV_COLLAPSE` with `do_madvise()`, I need to make sure `do_madvise()` correctly calls `madvise_lock()`. That does happen as a first step in `do_madvise()`, so we are good there.

I am looking now to `get_untagged_addr(mm, start)` in `madvise_do_behavior()`. For a remote process, `untagged_addr_remote(mm, start)` *does* check `mmap_read_lock()`.

I also see that `madvise_do_behavior()` is called AFTER `madvise_lock()` inside `do_madvise()`. So, the `mmap_lock` is held when `untagged_addr_remote()` is called. That means it's perfectly safe.

My next concern is around kernel thread restrictions for `do_madvise()`. `process_madvise()` contains a check to see if we're manipulating a remote process's address space. It uses `process_madvise_remote_valid()` to make this determination. Now, `process_madvise_remote_valid()` *does* allow `MADV_COLLAPSE`. But `damos_madvise()` in DAMON calls `do_madvise(mm, ...)` *directly*, bypassing this check! This is interesting and important.

DAMON already calls `do_madvise()` for `MADV_WILLNEED`, `MADV_COLD`, `MADV_PAGEOUT`, `MADV_HUGEPAGE`, and `MADV_NOHUGEPAGE`.

I should check what the implications of `MADV_HUGEPAGE` and `MADV_NOHUGEPAGE` are remotely. But `process_madvise_remote_valid()` doesn't allow those values.

So, since DAMON calls `do_madvise()` directly, there are no UAPI restrictions. Because this is an in-kernel operation defined by a root user (since DAMON sysfs requires root), it is fine.

Finally, I need to check for interactions between `khugepaged` and `MADV_COLLAPSE` inside `madvise_collapse()`.

    "thought": "**Analyzing MADV_COLLAPSE and DAMON's Interaction**

Okay, let's break down how `MADV_COLLAPSE` works and whether there are any unexpected consequences when used with DAMON. The core of this seems to be the lock manipulation, so let's check that aspect first.

My initial thought is to make sure there are no issues with `MADV_COLLAPSE` within `do_madvise()`. I need to ensure `madvise_should_skip()` isn't inadvertently bypassing anything, but it looks good so far; it just checks validity. It looks like `madvise_do_behavior()` calls `madvise_walk_vmas()`.

Inside `madvise_walk_vmas()`, we iterate through VMAs and, for the `MADV_COLLAPSE` case, call `madvise_collapse()`. Here, things are interesting. Could a partial success in collapsing huge pages with `MADV_COLLAPSE` cause problems for `do_madvise()`? `madvise_collapse()` returns an error code if some PMDs fail. It's the standard error return, right?

Indeed, `do_madvise()` returns this error code. In `damos_madvise()`, if `do_madvise()` returns an error, `applied` becomes `0`. DAMON uses this value for bytes applied. What if `do_madvise` succeeds partially? If `madvise_collapse()` partially succeeds but returns an error, `do_madvise` returns an error, `damos_madvise` returns `0`, and DAMON records `0` bytes applied. This seems consistent with other `madvise` behaviors where an error means `0` bytes applied, even if some work was done. Even `MADV_PAGEOUT` returns 0 if it walks the VMAs, not necessarily the number of pages paged out!

But here is the catch, `MADV_COLLAPSE` returns an error if *any* PMD fails. Even if most are successful! This means DAMON might report 0 bytes applied even when significant progress was made. Is this a real issue? It's a slight inaccuracy in DAMON stats, but not a huge deal.

Now, a larger issue to think about: While `damos_madvise` will simply return `0` if it gets an error from `do_madvise`, which can happen if `MADV_COLLAPSE` fails even slightly. I can see the CPU time spent collapsing other pages may not be reflected in DAMON's accounting.

Now, let's dig into the lock dropping inside `madvise_collapse()`. It calls `collapse_single_pmd()` and potentially drops the mmap lock. The `*lock_dropped` flag is set, and it handles it. It seems to reacquire the lock at the end before returning. This is important to note: `mmap_read_lock` is explicitly re-held before returning, meeting `madvise_walk_vmas`'s expectations.

The issue is this: `madvise_collapse()` drops the mmap lock. This invalidates the VMA iterator in `madvise_walk_vmas`!
Does `madvise_walk_vmas()` handle this for `MADV_COLLAPSE`?
I need to check how the VMA iteration is done. Is it safe to drop the lock and then reacquire it within the loop? This is something to investigate further.

If\n\t * traversing multiple VMAs, this is updated for each.\n\t */\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range range;\n\t/* The VMA and VMA preceding it (if applicable) currently targeted. */\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma;\n\tbool lock_dropped;\n};\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME\nstatic int madvise_walk_vmas(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior);\n\nstruct anon_vma_name *anon_vma_name_alloc(const char *name)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name;\n\tsize_t count;\n\n\t/* Add 1 for NUL terminator at the end of the anon_name->name */\n\tcount = strlen(name) + 1;\n\tanon_name = kmalloc_flex(*anon_name, name, count);\n\tif (anon_name) {\n\t\tkref_init(&anon_name->kref);\n\t\tmemcpy(anon_name->name, name, count);\n\t}\n\n\treturn anon_name;\n}\n\nvoid anon_vma_name_free(struct kref *kref)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name =\n\t\t\tcontainer_of(kref, struct anon_vma_name, kref);\n\tkfree(anon_name);\n}\n\nstruct anon_vma_name *anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\tvma_assert_stabilised(vma);\n\treturn vma->anon_name;\n}\n\n/* mmap_lock should be write-locked */\nstatic int replace_anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t struct anon_vma_name *anon_name)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *orig_name = anon_vma_name(vma);\n\n\tif (!anon_name) {\n\t\tvma->anon_name = NULL;\n\t\tanon_vma_name_put(orig_name);\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\tif (anon_vma_name_eq(orig_name, anon_name))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\tvma->anon_name = anon_vma_name_reuse(anon_name);\n\tanon_vma_name_put(orig_name);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n#else /* CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME */\nstatic int replace_anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t struct anon_vma_name *anon_name)\n{\n\tif (anon_name)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n#endif /* CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME */\n/*\n * Update the vm_flags or anon_name on region of a vma, splitting it or merging\n * it as necessary. Must be called with mmap_lock held for writing.\n */\nstatic int madvise_update_vma(vm_flags_t new_flags,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\tvma_flags_t new_vma_flags = legacy_to_vma_flags(new_flags);\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name = madv_behavior->anon_name;\n\tbool set_new_anon_name = madv_behavior->behavior == __MADV_SET_ANON_VMA_NAME;\n\tVMA_ITERATOR(vmi, madv_behavior->mm, range->start);\n\n\tif (vma_flags_same_mask(&vma->flags, new_vma_flags) &&\n\t    (!set_new_anon_name ||\n\t     anon_vma_name_eq(anon_vma_name(vma), anon_name)))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\tif (set_new_anon_name)\n\t\tvma = vma_modify_name(&vmi, madv_behavior->prev, vma,\n\t\t\trange->start, range->end, anon_name);\n\telse\n\t\tvma = vma_modify_flags(&vmi, madv_behavior->prev, vma,\n\t\t\trange->start, range->end, &new_vma_flags);\n\n\tif (IS_ERR(vma))\n\t\treturn PTR_ERR(vma);\n\n\t/*\n\t * If a new vma was created during vma_modify_XXX, the resulting\n\t * vma is already locked. Skip re-locking new vma in this case.\n\t */\n\tif (vma == madv_behavior->vma) {\n\t\tif (vma_start_write_killable(vma))\n\t\t\treturn -EINTR;\n\t} else {\n\t\tmadv_behavior->vma = vma;\n\t}\n\n\tvma->flags = new_vma_flags;\n\tif (set_new_anon_name)\n\t\treturn replace_anon_vma_name(vma, anon_name);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SWAP\nstatic int swapin_walk_pmd_entry(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long start,\n\t\tunsigned long end, struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->private;\n\tstruct swap_iocb *splug = NULL;\n\tpte_t *ptep = NULL;\n\tspinlock_t *ptl;\n\tunsigned long addr;\n\n\tfor (addr = start; addr < end; addr += PAGE_SIZE) {\n\t\tpte_t pte;\n\t\tsoftleaf_t entry;\n\t\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\t\tif (!ptep++) {\n\t\t\tptep = pte_offset_map_lock(vma->vm_mm, pmd, addr, &ptl);\n\t\t\tif (!ptep)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tpte = ptep_get(ptep);\n\t\tentry = softleaf_from_pte(pte);\n\t\tif (unlikely(!softleaf_is_swap(entry)))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\tpte_unmap_unlock(ptep, ptl);\n\t\tptep = NULL;\n\n\t\tfolio = read_swap_cache_async(entry, GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE,\n\t\t\t\t\t     vma, addr, &splug);\n\t\tif (folio)\n\t\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\t}\n\n\tif (ptep)\n\t\tpte_unmap_unlock(ptep, ptl);\n\tswap_read_unplug(splug);\n\tcond_resched();\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic const struct mm_walk_ops swapin_walk_ops = {\n\t.pmd_entry\t\t= swapin_walk_pmd_entry,\n\t.walk_lock\t\t= PGWALK_RDLOCK,\n};\n\nstatic void shmem_swapin_range(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\tunsigned long start, unsigned long end,\n\t\tstruct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, linear_page_index(vma, start));\n\tpgoff_t end_index = linear_page_index(vma, end) - 1;\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tstruct swap_iocb *splug = NULL;\n\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, end_index) {\n\t\tunsigned long addr;\n\t\tswp_entry_t entry;\n\n\t\tif (!xa_is_value(folio))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\tentry = radix_to_swp_entry(folio);\n\t\t/* There might be swapin error entries in shmem mapping. */\n\t\tif (!softleaf_is_swap(entry))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\taddr = vma->vm_start +\n\t\t\t((xas.xa_index - vma->vm_pgoff) << PAGE_SHIFT);\n\t\txas_pause(&xas);\n\t\trcu_read_unlock();\n\n\t\tfolio = read_swap_cache_async(entry, mapping_gfp_mask(mapping),\n\t\t\t\t\t     vma, addr, &splug);\n\t\tif (folio)\n\t\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\n\t\trcu_read_lock();\n\t}\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n\tswap_read_unplug(splug);\n}\n#endif\t\t/* CONFIG_SWAP */\n\nstatic void mark_mmap_lock_dropped(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tVM_WARN_ON_ONCE(madv_behavior->lock_mode == MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK);\n\tmadv_behavior->lock_dropped = true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Schedule all required I/O operations.  Do not wait for completion.\n */\nstatic long madvise_willneed(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tstruct file *file = vma->vm_file;\n\tunsigned long start = madv_behavior->range.start;\n\tunsigned long end = madv_behavior->range.end;\n\tloff_t offset;\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SWAP\n\tif (!file) {\n\t\twalk_page_range_vma(vma, start, end, &swapin_walk_ops, vma);\n\t\tlru_add_drain(); /* Push any new pages onto the LRU now */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\tif (shmem_mapping(file->f_mapping)) {\n\t\tshmem_swapin_range(vma, start, end, file->f_mapping);\n\t\tlru_add_drain(); /* Push any new pages onto the LRU now */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n#else\n\tif (!file)\n\t\treturn -EBADF;\n#endif\n\n\tif (IS_DAX(file_inode(file))) {\n\t\t/* no bad return value, but ignore advice */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Filesystem's fadvise may need to take various locks.  We need to\n\t * explicitly grab a reference because the vma (and hence the\n\t * vma's reference to the file) can go away as soon as we drop\n\t * mmap_lock.\n\t */\n\tmark_mmap_lock_dropped(madv_behavior);\n\tget_file(file);\n\toffset = (loff_t)(start - vma->vm_start)\n\t\t\t+ ((loff_t)vma->vm_pgoff << PAGE_SHIFT);\n\tmmap_read_unlock(mm);\n\tvfs_fadvise(file, offset, end - start, POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED);\n\tfput(file);\n\tmmap_read_lock(mm);\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic inline bool can_do_file_pageout(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\tif (!vma->vm_file)\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/*\n\t * paging out pagecache only for non-anonymous mappings that correspond\n\t * to the files the calling process could (if tried) open for writing;\n\t * otherwise we'd be including shared non-exclusive mappings, which\n\t * opens a side channel.\n\t */\n\treturn inode_owner_or_capable(&nop_mnt_idmap,\n\t\t\t\t      file_inode(vma->vm_file)) ||\n\t       file_permission(vma->vm_file, MAY_WRITE) == 0;\n}\n\nstatic inline int madvise_folio_pte_batch(unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t\t\t  struct folio *folio, pte_t *ptep,\n\t\t\t\t\t  pte_t *ptentp)\n{\n\tint max_nr = (end - addr) / PAGE_SIZE;\n\n\treturn folio_pte_batch_flags(folio, NULL, ptep, ptentp, max_nr,\n\t\t\t\t     FPB_MERGE_YOUNG_DIRTY);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_cold_or_pageout_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd,\n\t\t\t\tunsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t\tstruct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct madvise_walk_private *private = walk->private;\n\tstruct mmu_gather *tlb = private->tlb;\n\tbool pageout = private->pageout;\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = tlb->mm;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->vma;\n\tpte_t *start_pte, *pte, ptent;\n\tspinlock_t *ptl;\n\tstruct folio *folio = NULL;\n\tLIST_HEAD(folio_list);\n\tbool pageout_anon_only_filter;\n\tunsigned int batch_count = 0;\n\tint nr;\n\n\tif (fatal_signal_pending(current))\n\t\treturn -EINTR;\n\n\tpageout_anon_only_filter = pageout && !vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&\n\t\t\t\t\t!can_do_file_pageout(vma);\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE\n\tif (pmd_trans_huge(*pmd)) {\n\t\tpmd_t orig_pmd;\n\t\tunsigned long next = pmd_addr_end(addr, end);\n\n\t\ttlb_change_page_size(tlb, HPAGE_PMD_SIZE);\n\t\tptl = pmd_trans_huge_lock(pmd, vma);\n\t\tif (!ptl)\n\t\t\treturn 0;\n\n\t\torig_pmd = *pmd;\n\t\tif (is_huge_zero_pmd(orig_pmd))\n\t\t\tgoto huge_unlock;\n\n\t\tif (unlikely(!pmd_present(orig_pmd))) {\n\t\t\tVM_BUG_ON(thp_migration_supported() &&\n\t\t\t\t\t!pmd_is_migration_entry(orig_pmd));\n\n... [Diff truncated. Dropped 1455 lines] ...\n\n\tif (len_in && !len)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (start + len < start)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/*\n * madvise_should_skip() - Return if the request is invalid or nothing.\n * @start:\tStart address of madvise-requested address range.\n * @len_in:\tLength of madvise-requested address range.\n * @behavior:\tRequested madvise behavior.\n * @err:\tPointer to store an error code from the check.\n *\n * If the specified behaviour is invalid or nothing would occur, we skip the\n * operation.  This function returns true in the cases, otherwise false.  In\n * the former case we store an error on @err.\n */\nstatic bool madvise_should_skip(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tint behavior, int *err)\n{\n\tif (!is_valid_madvise(start, len_in, behavior)) {\n\t\t*err = -EINVAL;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\tif (start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in) == start) {\n\t\t*err = 0;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_madvise_populate(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_READ:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_WRITE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/*\n * untagged_addr_remote() assumes mmap_lock is already held. On\n * architectures like x86 and RISC-V, tagging is tricky because each\n * mm may have a different tagging mask. However, we might only hold\n * the per-VMA lock (currently only local processes are supported),\n * so untagged_addr is used to avoid the mmap_lock assertion for\n * local processes.\n */\nstatic inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,\n\t\tunsigned long start)\n{\n\treturn current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :\n\t\t\t\t   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_do_behavior(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct blk_plug plug;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\n\tif (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\trange->start = start;\n\t\trange->end = start + len_in;\n\t\treturn madvise_inject_error(madv_behavior);\n\t}\n\n\trange->start = get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start);\n\trange->end = range->start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in);\n\n\tblk_start_plug(&plug);\n\tif (is_madvise_populate(madv_behavior))\n\t\terror = madvise_populate(madv_behavior);\n\telse\n\t\terror = madvise_walk_vmas(madv_behavior);\n\tblk_finish_plug(&plug);\n\treturn error;\n}\n\n/*\n * The madvise(2) system call.\n *\n * Applications can use madvise() to advise the kernel how it should\n * handle paging I/O in this VM area.  The idea is to help the kernel\n * use appropriate read-ahead and caching techniques.  The information\n * provided is advisory only, and can be safely disregarded by the\n * kernel without affecting the correct operation of the application.\n *\n * behavior values:\n *  MADV_NORMAL - the default behavior is to read clusters.  This\n *\t\tresults in some read-ahead and read-behind.\n *  MADV_RANDOM - the system should read the minimum amount of data\n *\t\ton any access, since it is unlikely that the appli-\n *\t\tcation will need more than what it asks for.\n *  MADV_SEQUENTIAL - pages in the given range will probably be accessed\n *\t\tonce, so they can be aggressively read ahead, and\n *\t\tcan be freed soon after they are accessed.\n *  MADV_WILLNEED - the application is notifying the system to read\n *\t\tsome pages ahead.\n *  MADV_DONTNEED - the application is finished with the given range,\n *\t\tso the kernel can free resources associated with it.\n *  MADV_FREE - the application marks pages in the given range as lazy free,\n *\t\twhere actual purges are postponed until memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_REMOVE - the application wants to free up the given range of\n *\t\tpages and associated backing store.\n *  MADV_DONTFORK - omit this area from child's address space when forking:\n *\t\ttypically, to avoid COWing pages pinned by get_user_pages().\n *  MADV_DOFORK - cancel MADV_DONTFORK: no longer omit this area when forking.\n *  MADV_WIPEONFORK - present the child process with zero-filled memory in this\n *              range after a fork.\n *  MADV_KEEPONFORK - undo the effect of MADV_WIPEONFORK\n *  MADV_HWPOISON - trigger memory error handler as if the given memory range\n *\t\twere corrupted by unrecoverable hardware memory failure.\n *  MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE - try to soft-offline the given range of memory.\n *  MADV_MERGEABLE - the application recommends that KSM try to merge pages in\n *\t\tthis area with pages of identical content from other such areas.\n *  MADV_UNMERGEABLE- cancel MADV_MERGEABLE: no longer merge pages with others.\n *  MADV_HUGEPAGE - the application wants to back the given range by transparent\n *\t\thuge pages in the future. Existing pages might be coalesced and\n *\t\tnew pages might be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_NOHUGEPAGE - mark the given range as not worth being backed by\n *\t\ttransparent huge pages so the existing pages will not be\n *\t\tcoalesced into THP and new pages will not be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_COLLAPSE - synchronously coalesce pages into new THP.\n *  MADV_DONTDUMP - the application wants to prevent pages in the given range\n *\t\tfrom being included in its core dump.\n *  MADV_DODUMP - cancel MADV_DONTDUMP: no longer exclude from core dump.\n *  MADV_COLD - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tdeactivate pages in this range so that they can be reclaimed\n *\t\teasily if memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_PAGEOUT - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tpage out the pages in this range immediately.\n *  MADV_POPULATE_READ - populate (prefault) page tables readable by\n *\t\ttriggering read faults if required\n *  MADV_POPULATE_WRITE - populate (prefault) page tables writable by\n *\t\ttriggering write faults if required\n *\n * return values:\n *  zero    - success\n *  -EINVAL - start + len < 0, start is not page-aligned,\n *\t\t\"behavior\" is not a valid value, or application\n *\t\tis attempting to release locked or shared pages,\n *\t\tor the specified address range includes file, Huge TLB,\n *\t\tMAP_SHARED or VMPFNMAP range.\n *  -ENOMEM - addresses in the specified range are not currently\n *\t\tmapped, or are outside the AS of the process.\n *  -EIO    - an I/O error occurred while paging in data.\n *  -EBADF  - map exists, but area maps something that isn't a file.\n *  -EAGAIN - a kernel resource was temporarily unavailable.\n *  -EPERM  - memory is sealed.\n */\nint do_madvise(struct mm_struct *mm, unsigned long start, size_t len_in, int behavior)\n{\n\tint error;\n\tstruct mmu_gather tlb;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior madv_behavior = {\n\t\t.mm = mm,\n\t\t.behavior = behavior,\n\t\t.tlb = &tlb,\n\t};\n\n\tif (madvise_should_skip(start, len_in, behavior, &error))\n\t\treturn error;\n\terror = madvise_lock(&madv_behavior);\n\tif (error)\n\t\treturn error;\n\tmadvise_init_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\terror = madvise_do_behavior(start, len_in, &madv_behavior);\n\tmadvise_finish_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\tmadvise_unlock(&madv_behavior);\n\n\treturn error;\n}\n\nSYSCALL_DEFINE3(madvise, unsigned long, start, size_t, len_in, int, behavior)\n{\n\treturn do_madvise(current->mm, start, len_in, behavior);\n}\n\n/* Perform an madvise operation over a vector of addresses and lengths. */\nstatic ssize_t vector_madvise(struct mm_struct *mm, struct iov_iter *iter,\n\t\t\t      int behavior)\n{\n\tssize_t ret = 0;\n\tsize_t total_len;\n\tstruct mmu_gather tlb;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior madv_behavior = {\n\t\t.mm = mm,\n\t\t.behavior = behavior,\n\t\t.tlb = &tlb,\n\t};\n\n\ttotal_len = iov_iter_count(iter);\n\n\tret = madvise_lock(&madv_behavior);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\tmadvise_init_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\n\twhile (iov_iter_count(iter)) {\n\t\tunsigned long start = (unsigned long)iter_iov_addr(iter);\n\t\tsize_t len_in = iter_iov_len(iter);\n\t\tint error;\n\n\t\tif (madvise_should_skip(start, len_in, behavior, &error))\n\t\t\tret = error;\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tret = madvise_do_behavior(start, len_in, &madv_behavior);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * An madvise operation is attempting to restart the syscall,\n\t\t * but we cannot proceed as it would not be correct to repeat\n\t\t * the operation in aggregate, and would be surprising to the\n\t\t * user.\n\t\t *\n\t\t * We drop and reacquire locks so it is safe to just loop and\n\t\t * try again.     "thought": "**DAMON `MADV_COLLAPSE` Integration Analysis**

Okay, so I've been digging into this DAMON patch, and here's the gist of my analysis. First, I confirmed that `madvise_walk_vmas()` uses the correct VMA iteration, and the `find_vma()` calls are safe in the `madvise_collapse` path. This is good. It confirms that the `madvise_collapse` function, triggered by `do_madvise` through DAMON's `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` action, should be working correctly.

Then I checked the code shift in the Python selftest. The diff adds the new `collapse` action and re-numbers subsequent actions. The enums in `include/linux/damon.h` and the corresponding string representation in `damos_sysfs_action_names` and the Python selftest update are all consistent with the expected integer/string mapping. This is straightforward. No UAPI breakage because the sysfs interface uses strings.

The crux of the issue is that `MADV_COLLAPSE` is a synchronous operation. I initially worried that running this synchronously from `kdamond` might block the kernel thread and throw off DAMON's monitoring intervals. However, after carefully going through the code, I see that this is a *known* design choice with DAMON's synchronous actions, including `MADV_PAGEOUT` which can write pages to disk, and DAMON actions run synchronously. Users have the ability to throttle this with quotas (e.g. ms parameter). Also, `madvise_collapse` has built-in preemption in 2MB PMD steps with `cond_resched()` calls if the mmap lock is dropped during the collapse operation, which mitigates lockups. This is similar to existing behavior with other actions. And if THP is set to never, `MADV_COLLAPSE` will still work.

I then investigated the interaction with `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` being disabled, and it gracefully returns `-EINVAL`. This is safe. I checked the code for potential vulnerabilities. No null pointer dereferences, no list head issues, and no RCU-related problems were discovered.

The code seems solid, but there's a small potential gotcha. If `kdamond` is in a long `MADV_COLLAPSE` call on a massive region, it will take a while for `kthread_should_stop()` to be checked, which may cause latency. However, this isn't a bug. That's a fundamental property of running synchronous operations, and it can be controlled with quotas. And `madvise_collapse` calls `cond_resched()`, so this is mitigated.

Furthermore, it explicitly ignores the THP settings, making it possible to force collapse.

There are no missing capability checks, and the Python selftest aligns with the kernel enum.

Finally, a quick search for `DAMOS_STAT` confirmed that the relevant files have been updated, so there is no concern there.

So, in summary, based on my analysis, the patch introduces a new DAMON action which is safe, and the patch has handled edge cases well.

    "thought": "**DAMON Code Review: Collapsing VMA and Potential Gotchas**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was: in `damon_va_scheme_score` and `damon_pa_scheme_score`, `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` (which is newly added) defaults to `DAMOS_MAX_SCORE`. The core question becomes: why is this the intended behavior? I mean, shouldn't `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` be more discerning, prioritizing regions based on access characteristics, especially if a quota is specified?

After digging into `damon_va_scheme_score` and the other `damos` action scoring functions (`DAMOS_WILLNEED`, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`, etc.), I see that the only actions with custom scoring are `DAMOS_PAGEOUT` and the `MIGRATE` actions. This means that, by default, all other actions, including the new `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`, are treated equally during scheme application. This seems right, the `MAX_SCORE` signals that it's going to apply to *any* eligible region that matches the size and access constraint. This is because, ultimately, DAMON has a quota, and that quota has to get filled (or emptied) in the most efficient manner, which is the default implementation for those actions.

If a quota is specified, this `MAX_SCORE` means DAMON just applies the collapse action to regions that fit the criteria until the quota is exhausted. This is fine. But wait, if a user *does* try to tune things by specifying an access rate, the scoring does seem a bit random. Should we make `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` use `damon_hot_score()` (like `DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT`) or maybe `damon_cold_score()` to prioritize regions that are hot or cold? Leaving it as `MAX_SCORE` may result in less-than-optimal quota tuning via access rate, if that's a user's intent.

The good news is that this behavior of `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` returning `DAMOS_MAX_SCORE` is consistent with other actions like `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_WILLNEED`. At least the logic is internally consistent.

I then started thinking about filters. Are there any missing filter supports in applying `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`? There's support for `anon`, `file`, `young`, and `hugepage` filters in `damos_va_filter_out()` but the action is applied via `damos_madvise()`. Now, based on how the code works, it seems that there's an incompatibility here: `damos_madvise()` in `damon_va_apply_scheme` *doesn't* take into account the `sz_filter_passed` flags! In fact, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT`, `DAMOS_WILLNEED`, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`, and `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` all use `damos_madvise()`, which essentially bypasses the per-page filter logic, because it does *not* do a page-table walk. This leads to the obvious limitation: OPS-level filters aren't really supported for these actions. The design is that OPS filters only apply to operations with manual page table walks (like migrate, stat). `madvise`-based actions have a built in limitation. The commit message doesn't make any claims about OPS filter support, so we're not violating any implicit contract.

Now, looking at the updates on the selftest side, it looks like there's a new `collapse` action added to the `sysfs.py` selftest, which is good. No immediate memory leak or UAF concerns from this patch.

Next I wondered: could `MADV_COLLAPSE` potentially cause issues with `fvaddr` (Fixed Virtual Address monitoring)? No, `fvaddr` monitors a fixed virtual address range without dynamic updates.     "thought": "**DAMON with MADV_COLLAPSE Analysis**

Okay, let's break this down. The `damos_action` itself isn't directly traced by integer values that might break with the addition of `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`. Tracepoints export scheme indices instead. That's good. The sysfs interface relies on strings, and that part seems solid, confirming the `action_show` and the way the string is extracted for `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`.

The crucial thing is `MADV_COLLAPSE`'s interaction with `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`, since the code handles collapsing properly. Addresses passed to `do_madvise` are page-aligned and the region size is calculated safely, even if the starting address is not page-aligned. DAMON, in fact, behaves exactly like this with other `madvise` actions.

Livelock with `madvise_collapse()` in `khugepaged` seems unlikely because locking order is properly enforced with `mmap_read_lock`. The lock order is standard memory management procedure. The benchmarks using "THP set to never" and `DAMON action set to collapse` work correctly.

The sysfs parsing for `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` looks fine; it's added to the array correctly. `damon_pa_apply_scheme` correctly ignores it, returns `0`, which signifies no bytes were applied, similar to how `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE` works. This approach is consistent and safe. `mm/damon/reclaim.c` and `mm/damon/lru_sort.c` don't use `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`, so there's no interference. It integrates cleanly.

`MADV_COLLAPSE` is standard and architecture-agnostic. `madvise_collapse_errno()` has all the usual return values, and `damos_madvise` translates all non-zero values to 0 bytes applied. This is a common and safe practice.

Here's the problem: `damos_madvise` calls `do_madvise()`, and `do_madvise` might *partially* collapse a range and then return an error for the remainder (e.g., due to a failed allocation). The critical issue here is that `damos_madvise` may under-report the total bytes *actually* collapsed, and the code returns 0 when errors occur after partial completion. The code is relying on `do_madvise` to give the total that succeeded, but instead it is reporting the error. This is a design limitation of the API. However, DAMON correctly reports the applied memory for other `madvise` actions as well.

The lock order in `do_madvise` is safe; no apparent risks of livelock. The `BUG_ON`s in `madvise_collapse` are safe as `range->start` is always `>= vma->vm_start` and `range->end` is always `<= vma->vm_end`. The THP check with `thp_vma_allowable_order()` is as expected.

I have to verify the enum values in `sysfs.py` are updated. Good; the action ordering is now correct in the selftests. The enum is an internal C enum, not UAPI.

A potentially nasty issue: `kdamond` can get stuck in a tight loop if `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` is applied to regions that cannot be collapsed (e.g., due to memory fragmentation), because it will try to allocate and fail repeatedly. DAMON uses a quota system (bytes processed per interval) controlled by the user, and the time quota limits time spent by DAMON within an apply interval, but this quota is *between* region applications, and not *inside* `do_madvise()`. That means large regions, especially with uniform access, will pass large regions to `do_madvise` and could block `kdamond` for seconds or minutes. It will behave the same for `MADV_PAGEOUT`. DAMON does split regions dynamically, but those regions can still be large if the access pattern is uniform. The DAMON quota adjusts `sz_quota` (bytes allowed to be processed) based on the time quota! I need to double-check that part.

prev->vm_end;\n\n\t\tif (gap > sz_range(&first_gap)) {\n\t\t\tsecond_gap = first_gap;\n\t\t\tfirst_gap.start = prev->vm_end;\n\t\t\tfirst_gap.end = vma->vm_start;\n\t\t} else if (gap > sz_range(&second_gap)) {\n\t\t\tsecond_gap.start = prev->vm_end;\n\t\t\tsecond_gap.end = vma->vm_start;\n\t\t}\nnext:\n\t\tprev = vma;\n\t}\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n\n\tif (!sz_range(&second_gap) || !sz_range(&first_gap))\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\t/* Sort the two biggest gaps by address */\n\tif (first_gap.start > second_gap.start)\n\t\tswap(first_gap, second_gap);\n\n\t/* Store the result */\n\tregions[0].start = ALIGN(start, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\tregions[0].end = ALIGN(first_gap.start, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\tregions[1].start = ALIGN(first_gap.end, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\tregions[1].end = ALIGN(second_gap.start, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\tregions[2].start = ALIGN(second_gap.end, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\tregions[2].end = ALIGN(prev->vm_end, DAMON_MIN_REGION_SZ);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\n/*\n * Get the three regions in the given target (task)\n *\n * Returns 0 on success, negative error code otherwise.\n */\nstatic int damon_va_three_regions(struct damon_target *t,\n\t\t\t\tstruct damon_addr_range regions[3])\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm;\n\tint rc;\n\n\tmm = damon_get_mm(t);\n\tif (!mm)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\tmmap_read_lock(mm);\n\trc = __damon_va_three_regions(mm, regions);\n\tmmap_read_unlock(mm);\n\n\tmmput(mm);\n\treturn rc;\n}\n\n/*\n * Initialize the monitoring target regions for the given target (task)\n *\n * t\tthe given target\n *\n * Because only a number of small portions of the entire address space\n * is actually mapped to the memory and accessed, monitoring the unmapped\n * regions is wasteful.  That said, because we can deal with small noises,\n * tracking every mapping is not strictly required but could even incur a high\n * overhead if the mapping frequently changes or the number of mappings is\n * high.  The adaptive regions adjustment mechanism will further help to deal\n * with the noise by simply identifying the unmapped areas as a region that\n * has no access. That\n * said, too huge unmapped areas inside the monitoring target should be removed\n * to not take the time for the adaptive mechanism.\n *\n * For the reason, we convert the complex mappings to three distinct regions\n * that cover every mapped area of the address space. SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0\n\n===============\nDetailed Usages\n===============\n\nDAMON provides below interfaces for different users.\n\n- *Special-purpose DAMON modules.*\n  :ref:`This <damon_modules_special_purpose>` is for people who are building,\n  distributing, and/or administrating the kernel with special-purpose DAMON\n  usages.  Using this, users can use DAMON's major features for the given\n  purposes in build, boot, or runtime in simple ways.\n- *DAMON user space tool.*\n  `This <https://github.com/damonitor/damo>`_ is for privileged people such as\n  system administrators who want a just-working human-friendly interface.\n  Using this, users can use the DAMON’s major features in a human-friendly way.\n  It may not be highly tuned for special cases, though.  For more detail,\n  please refer to its `usage document\n  <https://github.com/damonitor/damo/blob/next/USAGE.md>`_.\n- *sysfs interface.*\n  :ref:`This <sysfs_interface>` is for privileged user space programmers who\n  want more optimized use of DAMON.  Using this, users can use DAMON’s major\n  features by reading from and writing to special sysfs files.  Therefore,\n  you can write and use your personalized DAMON sysfs wrapper programs that\n  reads/writes the sysfs files instead of you.  The `DAMON user space tool\n  <https://github.com/damonitor/damo>`_ is one example of such programs.\n- *Kernel Space Programming Interface.*\n  :doc:`This </mm/damon/api>` is for kernel space programmers.  Using this,\n  users can utilize every feature of DAMON most flexibly and efficiently by\n  writing kernel space DAMON application programs for you.  You can even extend\n  DAMON for various address spaces.  For detail, please refer to the interface\n  :doc:`document </mm/damon/api>`.\n\n.. _sysfs_interface:\n\nsysfs Interface\n===============\n\nDAMON sysfs interface is built when ``CONFIG_DAMON_SYSFS`` is defined.  It\ncreates multiple directories and files under its sysfs directory,\n``<sysfs>/kernel/mm/damon/``.  You can control DAMON by writing to and reading\nfrom the files under the directory.\n\nFor a short example, users can monitor the virtual address space of a given\nworkload as below. ::\n\n    # cd /sys/kernel/mm/damon/admin/\n    # echo 1 > kdamonds/nr_kdamonds && echo 1 > kdamonds/0/contexts/nr_contexts\n    # echo vaddr > kdamonds/0/contexts/0/operations\n    # echo 1 > kdamonds/0/contexts/0/targets/nr_targets\n    # echo $(pidof <workload>) > kdamonds/0/contexts/0/targets/0/pid_target\n    # echo on > kdamonds/0/state\n\nFiles Hierarchy\n---------------\n\nThe files hierarchy of DAMON sysfs interface is shown below.  In the below\nfigure, parents-children relations are represented with indentations, each\ndirectory is having ``/`` suffix, and files in each directory are separated by\ncomma (\",\").\n\n.. parsed-literal::\n\n    :ref:`/sys/kernel/mm/damon <sysfs_root>`/admin\n    │ :ref:`kdamonds <sysfs_kdamonds>`/nr_kdamonds\n    │ │ :ref:`0 <sysfs_kdamond>`/state,pid,refresh_ms\n    │ │ │ :ref:`contexts <sysfs_contexts>`/nr_contexts\n    │ │ │ │ :ref:`0 <sysfs_context>`/avail_operations,operations,addr_unit\n    │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`monitoring_attrs <sysfs_monitoring_attrs>`/\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ intervals/sample_us,aggr_us,update_us\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ intervals_goal/access_bp,aggrs,min_sample_us,max_sample_us\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ nr_regions/min,max\n    │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`targets <sysfs_targets>`/nr_targets\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`0 <sysfs_target>`/pid_target,obsolete_target\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`regions <sysfs_regions>`/nr_regions\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`0 <sysfs_region>`/start,end\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ...\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ ...\n    │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`schemes <sysfs_schemes>`/nr_schemes\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`0 <sysfs_scheme>`/action,target_nid,apply_interval_us\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`access_pattern <sysfs_access_pattern>`/\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ sz/min,max\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ nr_accesses/min,max\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ age/min,max\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`quotas <sysfs_quotas>`/ms,bytes,reset_interval_ms,effective_bytes,goal_tuner\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ weights/sz_permil,nr_accesses_permil,age_permil\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`goals <sysfs_schemes_quota_goals>`/nr_goals\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 0/target_metric,target_value,current_value,nid,path\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`watermarks <sysfs_watermarks>`/metric,interval_us,high,mid,low\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`{core_,ops_,}filters <sysfs_filters>`/nr_filters\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 0/type,matching,allow,memcg_path,addr_start,addr_end,target_idx,min,max\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`dests <damon_sysfs_dests>`/nr_dests\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 0/id,weight\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`stats <sysfs_schemes_stats>`/nr_tried,sz_tried,nr_applied,sz_applied,sz_ops_filter_passed,qt_exceeds,nr_snapshots,max_nr_snapshots\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ :ref:`tried_regions <sysfs_schemes_tried_regions>`/total_bytes\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 0/start,end,nr_accesses,age,sz_filter_passed\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ...\n    │ │ │ │ │ │ ...\n    │ │ │ │ ...\n    │ │ ...\n\n.. _sysfs_root:\n\nRoot\n----\n\nThe root of the DAMON sysfs interface is ``<sysfs>/kernel/mm/damon/``, and it\nhas one directory named ``admin``.  The directory contains the files for\nprivileged user space programs' control of DAMON.  User space tools or daemons\nhaving the root permission could use this directory.\n\n.. _sysfs_kdamonds:\n\nkdamonds/\n---------\n\nUnder the ``admin`` directory, one directory, ``kdamonds``, which has files for\ncontrolling the kdamonds (refer to\n:ref:`design <damon_design_execution_model_and_data_structures>` for more\ndetails) exists.  In the beginning, this directory has only one file,\n``nr_kdamonds``.  Writing a number (``N``) to the file creates the number of\nchild directories named ``0`` to ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each\nkdamond.\n\n.. _sysfs_kdamond:\n\nkdamonds/<N>/\n-------------\n\nIn each kdamond directory, three files (``state``, ``pid`` and ``refresh_ms``)\nand one directory (``contexts``) exist.\n\nReading ``state`` returns ``on`` if the kdamond is currently running, or\n``off`` if it is not running.\n\nUsers can write below commands for the kdamond to the ``state`` file.\n\n- ``on``: Start running.\n- ``off``: Stop running.\n- ``commit``: Read the user inputs in the sysfs files except ``state`` file\n  again.  Monitoring :ref:`target region <sysfs_regions>` inputs are also be\n  ignored if no target region is specified.\n- ``update_tuned_intervals``: Update the contents of ``sample_us`` and\n  ``aggr_us`` files of the kdamond with the auto-tuning applied ``sampling\n  interval`` and ``aggregation interval`` for the files.  Please refer to\n  :ref:`intervals_goal section <damon_usage_sysfs_monitoring_intervals_goal>`\n  for more details.\n- ``commit_schemes_quota_goals``: Read the DAMON-based operation schemes'\n  :ref:`quota goals <sysfs_schemes_quota_goals>`.\n- ``update_schemes_stats``: Update the contents of stats files for each\n  DAMON-based operation scheme of the kdamond.  For details of the stats,\n  please refer to :ref:`stats section <sysfs_schemes_stats>`.\n- ``update_schemes_tried_regions``: Update the DAMON-based operation scheme\n  action tried regions directory for each DAMON-based operation scheme of the\n  kdamond.  For details of the DAMON-based operation scheme action tried\n  regions directory, please refer to\n  :ref:`tried_regions section <sysfs_schemes_tried_regions>`.\n- ``update_schemes_tried_bytes``: Update only ``.../tried_regions/total_bytes``\n  files.\n- ``clear_schemes_tried_regions``: Clear the DAMON-based operating scheme\n  action tried regions directory for each DAMON-based operation scheme of the\n  kdamond.\n- ``update_schemes_effective_quotas``: Update the contents of\n  ``effective_bytes`` files for each DAMON-based operation scheme of the\n  kdamond.  For more details, refer to :ref:`quotas directory <sysfs_quotas>`.\n\nIf the state is ``on``, reading ``pid`` shows the pid of the kdamond thread.\n\nUsers can ask the kernel to periodically update files showing auto-tuned\nparameters and DAMOS stats instead of manually writing\n``update_tuned_intervals`` like keywords to ``state`` file.  For this, users\nshould write the desired update time interval in milliseconds to ``refresh_ms``\nfile.  If the interval is zero, the periodic update is disabled.  Reading the\nfile shows currently set time interval.\n\n``contexts`` directory contains files for controlling the monitoring contexts\nthat this kdamond will execute.\n\n.. _sysfs_contexts:\n\nkdamonds/<N>/contexts/\n----------------------\n\nIn the beginning, this directory has only one file, ``nr_contexts``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named as\n``0`` to ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each monitoring context (refer to\n:ref:`design <damon_design_execution_model_and_data_structures>` for more\ndetails).  At the moment, only one context per kdamond is supported, so only\n``0`` or ``1`` can be written to the file.\n\n.. _sysfs_context:\n\ncontexts/<N>/\n-------------\n\nIn each context directory, three files (``avail_operations``, ``operations``\nand ``addr_unit``) and three directories (``monitoring_attrs``, ``targets``,\nand ``schemes``) exist.\n\nDAMON supports multiple types of :ref:`monitoring operations\n<damon_design_configurable_operations_set>`, including those for virtual address\nspace and the physical address space.  You can get the list of available\nmonitoring operations set on the currently running kernel by reading\n``avail_operations`` file.  Based on the kernel configuration, the file will\nlist different available operation sets.  Please refer to the :ref:`design\n<damon_operations_set>` for the list of all available operation sets and their\nbrief explanations.\n\nYou can set and get what type of monitoring operations DAMON will use for the\ncontext by writing one of the keywords listed in ``avail_operations`` file and\nreading from the ``operations`` file.\n\n``addr_unit`` file is for setting and getting the :ref:`address unit\n<damon_design_addr_unit>` parameter of the operations set.\n\n.. _sysfs_monitoring_attrs:\n\ncontexts/<N>/monitoring_attrs/\n------------------------------\n\nFiles for specifying attributes of the monitoring including required quality\nand efficiency of the monitoring are in ``monitoring_attrs`` directory.\nSpecifically, two directories, ``intervals`` and ``nr_regions`` exist in this\ndirectory.\n\nUnder ``intervals`` directory, three files for DAMON's sampling interval\n(``sample_us``), aggregation interval (``aggr_us``), and update interval\n(``update_us``) exist.  You can set and get the values in micro-seconds by\nwriting to and reading from the files.\n\nUnder ``nr_regions`` directory, two files for the lower-bound and upper-bound\nof DAMON's monitoring regions (``min`` and ``max``, respectively), which\ncontrols the monitoring overhead, exist.  You can set and get the values by\nwriting to and rading from the files.\n\nFor more details about the intervals and monitoring regions range, please refer\nto the Design document (:doc:`/mm/damon/design`).\n\n.. _damon_usage_sysfs_monitoring_intervals_goal:\n\ncontexts/<N>/monitoring_attrs/intervals/intervals_goal/\n-------------------------------------------------------\n\nUnder the ``intervals`` directory, one directory for automated tuning of\n``sample_us`` and ``aggr_us``, namely ``intervals_goal`` directory also exists.\nUnder the directory, four files for the auto-tuning control, namely\n``access_bp``, ``aggrs``, ``min_sample_us`` and ``max_sample_us`` exist.\nPlease refer to  the :ref:`design document of the feature\n<damon_design_monitoring_intervals_autotuning>` for the internal of the tuning\nmechanism.  Reading and writing the four files under ``intervals_goal``\ndirectory shows and updates the tuning parameters that described in the\n:ref:design doc <damon_design_monitoring_intervals_autotuning>` with the same\nnames.  The tuning starts with the user-set ``sample_us`` and ``aggr_us``.  The\ntuning-applied current values of the two intervals can be read from the\n``sample_us`` and ``aggr_us`` files after writing ``update_tuned_intervals`` to\nthe ``state`` file.\n\n.. _sysfs_targets:\n\ncontexts/<N>/targets/\n---------------------\n\nIn the beginning, this directory has only one file, ``nr_targets``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each monitoring target.\n\n.. _sysfs_target:\n\ntargets/<N>/\n------------\n\nIn each target directory, two files (``pid_target`` and ``obsolete_target``)\nand one directory (``regions``) exist.\n\nIf you wrote ``vaddr`` to the ``contexts/<N>/operations``, each target should\nbe a process.  You can specify the process to DAMON by writing the pid of the\nprocess to the ``pid_target`` file.\n\nUsers can selectively remove targets in the middle of the targets array by\nwriting non-zero value to ``obsolete_target`` file and committing it (writing\n``commit`` to ``state`` file).  DAMON will remove the matching targets from its\ninternal targets array.  Users are responsible to construct target directories\nagain, so that those correctly represent the changed internal targets array.\n\n\n.. _sysfs_regions:\n\ntargets/<N>/regions\n-------------------\n\nIn case of ``fvaddr`` or ``paddr`` monitoring operations sets, users are\nrequired to set the monitoring target address ranges.  In case of ``vaddr``\noperations set, it is not mandatory, but users can optionally set the initial\nmonitoring region to specific address ranges.  Please refer to the :ref:`design\n<damon_design_vaddr_target_regions_construction>` for more details.\n\nFor such cases, users can explicitly set the initial monitoring target regions\nas they want, by writing proper values to the files under this directory.\n\nIn the beginning, this directory has only one file, ``nr_regions``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each initial monitoring target region.\n\nIf ``nr_regions`` is zero when committing new DAMON parameters online (writing\n``commit`` to ``state`` file of :ref:`kdamond <sysfs_kdamond>`), the commit\nlogic ignores the target regions.  In other words, the current monitoring\nresults for the target are preserved.\n\n.. _sysfs_region:\n\nregions/<N>/\n------------\n\nIn each region directory, you will find two files (``start`` and ``end``).  You\ncan set and get the start and end addresses of the initial monitoring target\nregion by writing to and reading from the files, respectively.\n\nEach region should not overlap with others.  ``end`` of directory ``N`` should\nbe equal or smaller than ``start`` of directory ``N+1``.\n\n.. _sysfs_schemes:\n\ncontexts/<N>/schemes/\n---------------------\n\nThe directory for DAMON-based Operation Schemes (:ref:`DAMOS\n<damon_design_damos>`).  Users can get and set the schemes by reading from and\nwriting to files under this directory.\n\nIn the beginning, this directory has only one file, ``nr_schemes``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each DAMON-based operation scheme.\n\n.. _sysfs_scheme:\n\nschemes/<N>/\n------------\n\nIn each scheme directory, eight directories (``access_pattern``, ``quotas``,\n``watermarks``, ``core_filters``, ``ops_filters``, ``filters``, ``dests``,\n``stats``, and ``tried_regions``) and three files (``action``, ``target_nid``\nand ``apply_interval``) exist.\n\nThe ``action`` file is for setting and getting the scheme's :ref:`action\n<damon_design_damos_action>`.  The keywords that can be written to and read\nfrom the file and their meaning are same to those of the list on\n:ref:`design doc <damon_design_damos_action>`.\n\nThe ``target_nid`` file is for setting the migration target node, which is\nonly meaningful when the ``action`` is either ``migrate_hot`` or\n``migrate_cold``.\n\nThe ``apply_interval_us`` file is for setting and getting the scheme's\n:ref:`apply_interval <damon_design_damos>` in microseconds.\n\n.. _sysfs_access_pattern:\n\nschemes/<N>/access_pattern/\n---------------------------\n\nThe directory for the target access :ref:`pattern\n<damon_design_damos_access_pattern>` of the given DAMON-based operation scheme.\n\nUnder the ``access_pattern`` directory, three directories (``sz``,\n``nr_accesses``, and ``age``) each having two files (``min`` and ``max``)\nexist.  You can set and get the access pattern for the given scheme by writing\nto and reading from the ``min`` and ``max`` files under ``sz``,\n``nr_accesses``, and ``age`` directories, respectively.  Note that the ``min``\nand the ``max`` form a closed interval.\n\n.. _sysfs_quotas:\n\nschemes/<N>/quotas/\n-------------------\n\nThe directory for the :ref:`quotas <damon_design_damos_quotas>` of the given\nDAMON-based operation scheme.\n\nUnder ``quotas`` directory, five files (``ms``, ``bytes``,\n``reset_interval_ms``, ``effective_bytes`` and ``goal_tuner``) and two\ndirectories (``weights`` and ``goals``) exist.\n\nYou can set the ``time quota`` in milliseconds, ``size quota`` in bytes, and\n``reset interval`` in milliseconds by writing the values to the three files,\nrespectively.  Then, DAMON tries to use only up to ``time quota`` milliseconds\nfor applying the ``action`` to memory regions of the ``access_pattern``, and to\napply the action to only up to ``bytes`` bytes of memory regions within the\n``reset_interval_ms``.  Setting both ``ms`` and ``bytes`` zero disables the\nquota limits unless at least one :ref:`goal <sysfs_schemes_quota_goals>` is\nset.\n\nYou can set the goal-based effective quota auto-tuning algorithm to use, by\nwriting the algorithm name to ``goal_tuner`` file.  Reading the file returns\nthe currently selected tuner algorithm.  Refer to the design documentation of\n:ref:`automatic quota tuning goals <damon_design_damos_quotas_auto_tuning>` for\nthe background design of the feature and the name of the selectable algorithms.\nRefer to :ref:`goals directory <sysfs_schemes_quota_goals>` for the goals\nsetup.\n\nThe time quota is internally transformed to a size quota.  Between the\ntransformed size quota and user-specified size quota, smaller one is applied.\nBased on the user-specified :ref:`goal <sysfs_schemes_quota_goals>`, the\neffective size quota is further adjusted.  Reading ``effective_bytes`` returns\nthe current effective size quota.  The file is not updated in real time, so\nusers should ask DAMON sysfs interface to update the content of the file for\nthe stats by writing a special keyword, ``update_schemes_effective_quotas`` to\nthe relevant ``kdamonds/<N>/state`` file.\n\nUnder ``weights`` directory, three files (``sz_permil``,\n``nr_accesses_permil``, and ``age_permil``) exist.\nYou can set the :ref:`prioritization weights\n<damon_design_damos_quotas_prioritization>` for size, access frequency, and age\nin per-thousand unit by writing the values to the three files under the\n``weights`` directory.\n\n.. _sysfs_schemes_quota_goals:\n\nschemes/<N>/quotas/goals/\n-------------------------\n\nThe directory for the :ref:`automatic quota tuning goals\n<damon_design_damos_quotas_auto_tuning>` of the given DAMON-based operation\nscheme.\n\nIn the beginning, this directory has only one file, ``nr_goals``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each goal and current achievement.\nAmong the multiple feedback, the best one is used.\n\nEach goal directory contains five files, namely ``target_metric``,\n``target_value``, ``current_value`` ``nid`` and ``path``.  Users can set and\nget the five parameters for the quota auto-tuning goals that specified on the\n:ref:`design doc <damon_design_damos_quotas_auto_tuning>` by writing to and\nreading from each of the files.  Note that users should further write\n``commit_schemes_quota_goals`` to the ``state`` file of the :ref:`kdamond\ndirectory <sysfs_kdamond>` to pass the feedback to DAMON.\n\n.. _sysfs_watermarks:\n\nschemes/<N>/watermarks/\n-----------------------\n\nThe directory for the :ref:`watermarks <damon_design_damos_watermarks>` of the\ngiven DAMON-based operation scheme.\n\nUnder the watermarks directory, five files (``metric``, ``interval_us``,\n``high``, ``mid``, and ``low``) for setting the metric, the time interval\nbetween check of the metric, and the three watermarks exist.  You can set and\nget the five values by writing to the files, respectively.\n\nKeywords and meanings of those that can be written to the ``metric`` file are\nas below.\n\n - none: Ignore the watermarks\n - free_mem_rate: System's free memory rate (per thousand)\n\nThe ``interval`` should written in microseconds unit.\n\n.. _sysfs_filters:\n\nschemes/<N>/{core\\_,ops\\_,}filters/\n-----------------------------------\n\nDirectories for :ref:`filters <damon_design_damos_filters>` of the given\nDAMON-based operation scheme.\n\n``core_filters`` and ``ops_filters`` directories are for the filters handled by\nthe DAMON core layer and operations set layer, respectively.  ``filters``\ndirectory can be used for installing filters regardless of their handled\nlayers.  Filters that requested by ``core_filters`` and ``ops_filters`` will be\ninstalled before those of ``filters``.  All three directories have same files.\n\nUse of ``filters`` directory can make expecting evaluation orders of given\nfilters with the files under directory bit confusing.  Users are hence\nrecommended to use ``core_filters`` and ``ops_filters`` directories.  The\n``filters`` directory could be deprecated in future.\n\nIn the beginning, the directory has only one file, ``nr_filters``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each filter.  The filters are evaluated\nin the numeric order.\n\nEach filter directory contains nine files, namely ``type``, ``matching``,\n``allow``, ``memcg_path``, ``addr_start``, ``addr_end``, ``min``, ``max``\nand ``target_idx``.  To ``type`` file, you can write the type of the filter.\nRefer to :ref:`the design doc <damon_design_damos_filters>` for available type\nnames, their meaning and on what layer those are handled.\n\nFor ``memcg`` type, you can specify the memory cgroup of the interest by\nwriting the path of the memory cgroup from the cgroups mount point to\n``memcg_path`` file.  For ``addr`` type, you can specify the start and end\naddress of the range (open-ended interval) to ``addr_start`` and ``addr_end``\nfiles, respectively.  For ``hugepage_size`` type, you can specify the minimum\nand maximum size of the range (closed interval) to ``min`` and ``max`` files,\nrespectively.  For ``target`` type, you can specify the index of the target\nbetween the list of the DAMON context's monitoring targets list to\n``target_idx`` file.\n\nYou can write ``Y`` or ``N`` to ``matching`` file to specify whether the filter\nis for memory that matches the ``type``.  You can write ``Y`` or ``N`` to\n``allow`` file to specify if applying the action to the memory that satisfies\nthe ``type`` and ``matching`` should be allowed or not.\n\nFor example, below restricts a DAMOS action to be applied to only non-anonymous\npages of all memory cgroups except ``/having_care_already``.::\n\n    # cd ops_filters/0/\n    # echo 2 > nr_filters\n    # # disallow anonymous pages\n    echo anon > 0/type\n    echo Y > 0/matching\n    echo N > 0/allow\n    # # further filter out all cgroups except one at '/having_care_already'\n    echo memcg > 1/type\n    echo /having_care_already > 1/memcg_path\n    echo Y > 1/matching\n    echo N > 1/allow\n\nRefer to the :ref:`DAMOS filters design documentation\n<damon_design_damos_filters>` for more details including how multiple filters\nof different ``allow`` works, when each of the filters are supported, and\ndifferences on stats.\n\n.. _damon_sysfs_dests:\n\nschemes/<N>/dests/\n------------------\n\nDirectory for specifying the destinations of given DAMON-based operation\nscheme's action.  This directory is ignored if the action of the given scheme\nis not supporting multiple destinations.  Only ``DAMOS_MIGRATE_{HOT,COLD}``\nactions are supporting multiple destinations.\n\nIn the beginning, the directory has only one file, ``nr_dests``.  Writing a\nnumber (``N``) to the file creates the number of child directories named ``0``\nto ``N-1``.  Each directory represents each action destination.\n\nEach destination directory contains two files, namely ``id`` and ``weight``.\nUsers can write and read the identifier of the destination to ``id`` file.\nFor ``DAMOS_MIGRATE_{HOT,COLD}`` actions, the migrate destination node's node\nid should be written to ``id`` file.  Users can write and read the weight of\nthe destination among the given destinations to the ``weight`` file.  The\nweight can be an arbitrary integer.  When DAMOS apply the action to each entity\nof the memory region, it will select the destination of the action based on the\nrelative weights of the destinations.\n\n.. _sysfs_schemes_stats:\n\nschemes/<N>/stats/\n------------------\n\nDAMON counts statistics for each scheme.  This statistics can be used for\nonline analysis or tuning of the schemes.  Refer to :ref:`design doc\n<damon_design_damos_stat>` for more details about the stats.\n\nThe statistics can be retrieved by reading the files under ``stats`` directory\n(``nr_tried``, ``sz_tried``, ``nr_applied``, ``sz_applied``,\n``sz_ops_filter_passed``, ``qt_exceeds``, ``nr_snapshots`` and\n``max_nr_snapshots``), respectively.\n\nThe files are not updated in real time by default.  Users should ask DAMON\nsysfs interface to periodically update those using ``refresh_ms``, or do a one\ntime update by writing a special keyword, ``update_schemes_stats`` to the\nrelevant ``kdamonds/<N>/state`` file.  Refer to :ref:`kdamond directory\n<sysfs_kdamond>` for more details.\n\n.. _sysfs_schemes_tried_regions:\n\nschemes/<N>/tried_regions/\n--------------------------\n\nThis directory initially has one file, ``total_bytes``.\n\nWhen a special keyword, ``update_schemes_tried_regions``, is written to the\nrelevant ``kdamonds/<N>/state`` file, DAMON updates the ``total_bytes`` file so\nthat reading it returns the total size of the scheme tried regions, and creates\ndirectories named integer starting from ``0`` under this directory.  Each\ndirectory contains files exposing detailed information about each of the memory\nregion that the corresponding scheme's ``action`` has tried to be applied under\nthis directory, during next :ref:`apply interval <damon_design_damos>` of the\ncorresponding scheme.  The information includes address range, ``nr_accesses``,\nand ``age`` of the region.\n\nWriting ``update_schemes_tried_bytes`` to the relevant ``kdamonds/<N>/state``\nfile will only update the ``total_bytes`` file, and will not create the\nsubdirectories.\n\nThe directories will be removed when another special keyword,\n``clear_schemes_tried_regions``, is written to the relevant\n``kdamonds/<N>/state`` file.\n\nThe expected usage of this directory is investigations of schemes' behaviors,\nand query-like efficient data access monitoring results retrievals.  For the\nlatter use case, in particular, users can set the ``action`` as ``stat`` and\nset the ``access pattern`` as their interested pattern that they want to query.\n\n.. _sysfs_schemes_tried_region:\n\ntried_regions/<N>/\n------------------\n\nIn each region directory, you will find five files (``start``, ``end``,\n``nr_accesses``, ``age``, and ``sz_filter_passed``).  Reading the files will\nshow the properties of the region that corresponding DAMON-based operation\nscheme ``action`` has tried to be applied.\n\nExample\n~~~~~~~\n\nBelow commands applies a scheme saying \"If a memory region of size in [4KiB,\n8KiB] is showing accesses per aggregate interval in [0, 5] for aggregate\ninterval in [10, 20], page out the region.  For the paging out, use only up to\n10ms per second, and also don't page out more than 1GiB per second.  Under the\nlimitation, page out memory regions having longer age first.  Also, check the\nfree memory rate of the system every 5 seconds, start the monitoring and paging\nout when the free memory rate becomes lower than 50%, but stop it if the free\nmemory rate becomes larger than 60%, or lower than 30%\". ::\n\n    # cd <sysfs>/kernel/mm/damon/admin\n    # # populate directories\n    # echo 1 > kdamonds/nr_kdamonds; echo 1 > kdamonds/0/contexts/nr_contexts;\n    # echo 1 > kdamonds/0/contexts/0/schemes/nr_schemes\n    # cd kdamonds/0/contexts/0/schemes/0\n    # # set the basic access pattern and the action\n    # echo 4096 > access_pattern/sz/min\n    # echo 8192 > access_pattern/sz/max\n    # echo 0 > access_pattern/nr_accesses/min\n    # echo 5 > access_pattern/nr_accesses/max\n    # echo 10 > access_pattern/age/min\n    # echo 20 > access_pattern/age/max\n    # echo pageout > action\n    # # set quotas\n    # echo 10 > quotas/ms\n    # echo $((1024*1024*1024)) > quotas/bytes\n    # echo 1000 > quotas/reset_interval_ms\n    # # set watermark\n    # echo free_mem_rate > watermarks/metric\n    # echo 5000000 > watermarks/interval_us\n    # echo 600 > watermarks/high\n    # echo 500 > watermarks/mid\n    # echo 300 > watermarks/low\n\nPlease note that it's highly recommended to use user space tools like `damo\n<https://github.com/damonitor/damo>`_ rather than manually reading and writing\nthe files as above.  Above is only for an example.\n\n.. _tracepoint:\n\nTracepoints for Monitoring Results\n==================================\n\nUsers can get the monitoring results via the :ref:`tried_regions\n<sysfs_schemes_tried_regions>`.  The interface is useful for getting a\nsnapshot, but it could be inefficient for fully recording all the monitoring\nresults.  For the purpose, two trace points, namely ``damon:damon_aggregated``\nand ``damon:damos_before_apply``, are provided.  ``damon:damon_aggregated``\nprovides the whole monitoring results, while ``damon:damos_before_apply``\nprovides the monitoring results for regions that each DAMON-based Operation\nScheme (:ref:`DAMOS <damon_design_damos>`) is gonna be applied.  Hence,\n``damon:damos_before_apply`` is more useful for recording internal behavior of\nDAMOS, or DAMOS target access\n:ref:`pattern <damon_design_damos_access_pattern>` based query-like efficient\nmonitoring results recording.\n\nWhile the monitoring is turned on, you could record the tracepoint events and\nshow results using tracepoint supporting tools like ``perf``.  For example::\n\n    # echo on > kdamonds/0/state\n    # perf record -e damon:damon_aggregated &\n    # sleep 5\n    # kill 9 $(pidof perf)\n    # echo off > kdamonds/0/state\n    # perf script\n    kdamond.0 46568 [027] 79357.842179: damon:damon_aggregated: target_id=0 nr_regions=11 122509119488-135708762112: 0 864\n    [...]\n\nEach line of the perf script output represents each monitoring region.  The\nfirst five fields are as usual other tracepoint outputs.  The sixth field\n(``target_id=X``) shows the ide of the monitoring target of the region.  The\nseventh field (``nr_regions=X``) shows the total number of monitoring regions\nfor the target.  The eighth field (``X-Y:``) shows the start (``X``) and end\n(``Y``) addresses of the region in bytes.  The ninth field (``X``) shows the\n``nr_accesses`` of the region (refer to\n:ref:`design <damon_design_region_based_sampling>` for more details of the\ncounter).  Finally the tenth field (``X``) shows the ``age`` of the region\n(refer to :ref:`design <damon_design_age_tracking>` for more details of the\ncounter).\n\nIf the event was ``damon:damos_beofre_apply``, the ``perf script`` output would\nbe somewhat like below::\n\n    kdamond.0 47293 [000] 80801.060214: damon:damos_before_apply: ctx_idx=0 scheme_idx=0 target_idx=0 nr_regions=11 121932607488-135128711168: 0 136\n    [...]\n\nEach line of the output represents each monitoring region that each DAMON-based\nOperation Scheme was about to be applied at the traced time.  The first five\nfields are as usual.  It shows the index of the DAMON context (``ctx_idx=X``)\nof the scheme in the list of the contexts of the context's kdamond, the index\nof the scheme (``scheme_idx=X``) in the list of the schemes of the context, in\naddition to the output of ``damon_aggregated`` tracepoint.\n"}",

Okay, hold on a second! This `action` file description... it refers to the design doc for the action details. Let me check... *yes*, the design document was updated in that patch! Good, the documentation seems correct and in sync.

Now, the question is, did the code keep up? Is the API complete? I need to verify if the "collapse" action is implemented. The code points to `damos_madvise()` in `mm/damon/vaddr.c`. Let's take a look...

```c
static unsigned long damos_madvise(struct damon_target *target,
		struct damon_region *r, int behavior)
{
	struct mm_struct *mm;
	unsigned long start = PAGE_ALIGN(r->ar.start);
	unsigned long len = PAGE_ALIGN(damon_sz_region(r));
	unsigned long applied;

	mm = damon_get_mm(target);
	if (!mm)
		return 0;

	applied = do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior) ? 0 : len;
	mmput(mm);

	return applied;
}
```

Wait a minute... I don't see any explicit `mmap_read_lock` or `mmap_write_lock` being held *before* the call to `do_madvise()`. This is concerning. We're directly calling `do_madvise()` on the `mm` without any locking. I need to investigate `do_madvise()` itself to understand how it handles the mmap lock internally. This could be a race condition waiting to happen if the lock isn't managed correctly.
