    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0\n/*\n *\tlinux/mm/madvise.c\n *\n * Copyright (C) 1999  Linus Torvalds\n * Copyright (C) 2002  Christoph Hellwig\n */\n\n#include <linux/mman.h>\n#include <linux/pagemap.h>\n#include <linux/syscalls.h>\n#include <linux/mempolicy.h>\n#include <linux/page-isolation.h>\n#include <linux/page_idle.h>\n#include <linux/userfaultfd_k.h>\n#include <linux/hugetlb.h>\n#include <linux/falloc.h>\n#include <linux/fadvise.h>\n#include <linux/sched.h>\n#include <linux/sched/mm.h>\n#include <linux/mm_inline.h>\n#include <linux/mmu_context.h>\n#include <linux/string.h>\n#include <linux/uio.h>\n#include <linux/ksm.h>\n#include <linux/fs.h>\n#include <linux/file.h>\n#include <linux/blkdev.h>\n#include <linux/backing-dev.h>\n#include <linux/pagewalk.h>\n#include <linux/swap.h>\n#include <linux/leafops.h>\n#include <linux/shmem_fs.h>\n#include <linux/mmu_notifier.h>\n\n#include <asm/tlb.h>\n\n#include \"internal.h\"\n#include \"swap.h\"\n\n#define __MADV_SET_ANON_VMA_NAME (-1)\n\n/*\n * Maximum number of attempts we make to install guard pages before we give up\n * and return -ERESTARTNOINTR to have userspace try again.\n */\n#define MAX_MADVISE_GUARD_RETRIES 3\n\nstruct madvise_walk_private {\n\tstruct mmu_gather *tlb;\n\tbool pageout;\n};\n\nenum madvise_lock_mode {\n\tMADVISE_NO_LOCK,\n\tMADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK,\n\tMADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK,\n\tMADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK,\n};\n\nstruct madvise_behavior_range {\n\tunsigned long start;\n\tunsigned long end;\n};\n\nstruct madvise_behavior {\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm;\n\tint behavior;\n\tstruct mmu_gather *tlb;\n\tenum madvise_lock_mode lock_mode;\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name;\n\n\t/*\n\t * The range over which the behaviour is currently being applied. If\n\t * traversing multiple VMAs, this is updated for each.\n\t */\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range range;\n\t/* The VMA and VMA preceding it (if applicable) currently targeted. */\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma;\n\tbool lock_dropped;\n};\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME\nstatic int madvise_walk_vmas(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior);\n\nstruct anon_vma_name *anon_vma_name_alloc(const char *name)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name;\n\tsize_t count;\n\n\t/* Add 1 for NUL terminator at the end of the anon_name->name */\n\tcount = strlen(name) + 1;\n\tanon_name = kmalloc_flex(*anon_name, name, count);\n\tif (anon_name) {\n\t\tkref_init(&anon_name->kref);\n\t\tmemcpy(anon_name->name, name, count);\n\t}\n\n\treturn anon_name;\n}\n\nvoid anon_vma_name_free(struct kref *kref)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name =\n\t\t\tcontainer_of(kref, struct anon_vma_name, kref);\n\tkfree(anon_name);\n}\n\nstruct anon_vma_name *anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\tvma_assert_stabilised(vma);\n\treturn vma->anon_name;\n}\n\n/* mmap_lock should be write-locked */\nstatic int replace_anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t struct anon_vma_name *anon_name)\n{\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *orig_name = anon_vma_name(vma);\n\n\tif (!anon_name) {\n\t\tvma->anon_name = NULL;\n\t\tanon_vma_name_put(orig_name);\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\tif (anon_vma_name_eq(orig_name, anon_name))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\tvma->anon_name = anon_vma_name_reuse(anon_name);\n\tanon_vma_name_put(orig_name);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n#else /* CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME */\nstatic int replace_anon_vma_name(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t struct anon_vma_name *anon_name)\n{\n\tif (anon_name)\n\t\treturn -EINVAL;\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n#endif /* CONFIG_ANON_VMA_NAME */\n/*\n * Update the vm_flags or anon_name on region of a vma, splitting it or merging\n * it as necessary. Must be called with mmap_lock held for writing.\n */\nstatic int madvise_update_vma(vm_flags_t new_flags,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\tvma_flags_t new_vma_flags = legacy_to_vma_flags(new_flags);\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\tstruct anon_vma_name *anon_name = madv_behavior->anon_name;\n\tbool set_new_anon_name = madv_behavior->behavior == __MADV_SET_ANON_VMA_NAME;\n\tVMA_ITERATOR(vmi, madv_behavior->mm, range->start);\n\n\tif (vma_flags_same_mask(&vma->flags, new_vma_flags) &&\n\t    (!set_new_anon_name ||\n\t     anon_vma_name_eq(anon_vma_name(vma), anon_name)))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\tif (set_new_anon_name)\n\t\tvma = vma_modify_name(&vmi, madv_behavior->prev, vma,\n\t\t\trange->start, range->end, anon_name);\n\telse\n\t\tvma = vma_modify_flags(&vmi, madv_behavior->prev, vma,\n\t\t\trange->start, range->end, &new_vma_flags);\n\n\tif (IS_ERR(vma))\n\t\treturn PTR_ERR(vma);\n\n\t/*\n\t * If a new vma was created during vma_modify_XXX, the resulting\n\t * vma is already locked. Skip re-locking new vma in this case.\n\t */\n\tif (vma == madv_behavior->vma) {\n\t\tif (vma_start_write_killable(vma))\n\t\t\treturn -EINTR;\n\t} else {\n\t\tmadv_behavior->vma = vma;\n\t}\n\n\tvma->flags = new_vma_flags;\n\tif (set_new_anon_name)\n\t\treturn replace_anon_vma_name(vma, anon_name);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SWAP\nstatic int swapin_walk_pmd_entry(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long start,\n\t\tunsigned long end, struct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->private;\n\tstruct swap_iocb *splug = NULL;\n\tpte_t *ptep = NULL;\n\tspinlock_t *ptl;\n\tunsigned long addr;\n\n\tfor (addr = start; addr < end; addr += PAGE_SIZE) {\n\t\tpte_t pte;\n\t\tsoftleaf_t entry;\n\t\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\t\tif (!ptep++) {\n\t\t\tptep = pte_offset_map_lock(vma->vm_mm, pmd, addr, &ptl);\n\t\t\tif (!ptep)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tpte = ptep_get(ptep);\n\t\tentry = softleaf_from_pte(pte);\n\t\tif (unlikely(!softleaf_is_swap(entry)))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\tpte_unmap_unlock(ptep, ptl);\n\t\tptep = NULL;\n\n\t\tfolio = read_swap_cache_async(entry, GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE,\n\t\t\t\t\t     vma, addr, &splug);\n\t\tif (folio)\n\t\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\t}\n\n\tif (ptep)\n\t\tpte_unmap_unlock(ptep, ptl);\n\tswap_read_unplug(splug);\n\tcond_resched();\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic const struct mm_walk_ops swapin_walk_ops = {\n\t.pmd_entry\t\t= swapin_walk_pmd_entry,\n\t.walk_lock\t\t= PGWALK_RDLOCK,\n};\n\nstatic void shmem_swapin_range(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\tunsigned long start, unsigned long end,\n\t\tstruct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, linear_page_index(vma, start));\n\tpgoff_t end_index = linear_page_index(vma, end) - 1;\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tstruct swap_iocb *splug = NULL;\n\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, end_index) {\n\t\tunsigned long addr;\n\t\tswp_entry_t entry;\n\n\t\tif (!xa_is_value(folio))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\tentry = radix_to_swp_entry(folio);\n\t\t/* There might be swapin error entries in shmem mapping. */\n\t\tif (!softleaf_is_swap(entry))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\taddr = vma->vm_start +\n\t\t\t((xas.xa_index - vma->vm_pgoff) << PAGE_SHIFT);\n\t\txas_pause(&xas);\n\t\trcu_read_unlock();\n\n\t\tfolio = read_swap_cache_async(entry, mapping_gfp_mask(mapping),\n\t\t\t\t\t     vma, addr, &splug);\n\t\tif (folio)\n\t\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\n\t\trcu_read_lock();\n\t}\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n\tswap_read_unplug(splug);\n}\n#endif\t\t/* CONFIG_SWAP */\n\nstatic void mark_mmap_lock_dropped(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tVM_WARN_ON_ONCE(madv_behavior->lock_mode == MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK);\n\tmadv_behavior->lock_dropped = true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Schedule all required I/O operations.  Do not wait for completion.\n */\nstatic long madvise_willneed(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tstruct file *file = vma->vm_file;\n\tunsigned long start = madv_behavior->range.start;\n\tunsigned long end = madv_behavior->range.end;\n\tloff_t offset;\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SWAP\n\tif (!file) {\n\t\twalk_page_range_vma(vma, start, end, &swapin_walk_ops, vma);\n\t\tlru_add_drain(); /* Push any new pages onto the LRU now */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\tif (shmem_mapping(file->f_mapping)) {\n\t\tshmem_swapin_range(vma, start, end, file->f_mapping);\n\t\tlru_add_drain(); /* Push any new pages onto the LRU now */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n#else\n\tif (!file)\n\t\treturn -EBADF;\n#endif\n\n\tif (IS_DAX(file_inode(file))) {\n\t\t/* no bad return value, but ignore advice */\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Filesystem's fadvise may need to take various locks.  We need to\n\t * explicitly grab a reference because the vma (and hence the\n\t * vma's reference to the file) can go away as soon as we drop\n\t * mmap_lock.\n\t */\n\tmark_mmap_lock_dropped(madv_behavior);\n\tget_file(file);\n\toffset = (loff_t)(start - vma->vm_start)\n\t\t\t+ ((loff_t)vma->vm_pgoff << PAGE_SHIFT);\n\tmmap_read_unlock(mm);\n\tvfs_fadvise(file, offset, end - start, POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED);\n\tfput(file);\n\tmmap_read_lock(mm);\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic inline bool can_do_file_pageout(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\tif (!vma->vm_file)\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/*\n\t * paging out pagecache only for non-anonymous mappings that correspond\n\t * to the files the calling process could (if tried) open for writing;\n\t * otherwise we'd be including shared non-exclusive mappings, which\n\t * opens a side channel.\n\t */\n\treturn inode_owner_or_capable(&nop_mnt_idmap,\n\t\t\t\t      file_inode(vma->vm_file)) ||\n\t       file_permission(vma->vm_file, MAY_WRITE) == 0;\n}\n\nstatic inline int madvise_folio_pte_batch(unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t\t\t  struct folio *folio, pte_t *ptep,\n\t\t\t\t\t  pte_t *ptentp)\n{\n\tint max_nr = (end - addr) / PAGE_SIZE;\n\n\treturn folio_pte_batch_flags(folio, NULL, ptep, ptentp, max_nr,\n\t\t\t\t     FPB_MERGE_YOUNG_DIRTY);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_cold_or_pageout_pte_range(pmd_t *pmd,\n\t\t\t\tunsigned long addr, unsigned long end,\n\t\t\t\tstruct mm_walk *walk)\n{\n\tstruct madvise_walk_private *private = walk->private;\n\tstruct mmu_gather *tlb = private->tlb;\n\tbool pageout = private->pageout;\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = tlb->mm;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = walk->vma;\n\tpte_t *start_pte, *pte, ptent;\n\tspinlock_t *ptl;\n\tstruct folio *folio = NULL;\n\tLIST_HEAD(folio_list);\n\tbool pageout_anon_only_filter;\n\tunsigned int batch_count = 0;\n\tint nr;\n\n\tif (fatal_signal_pending(current))\n\t\treturn -EINTR;\n\n\tpageout_anon_only_filter = pageout && !vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&\n\t\t\t\t\t!can_do_file_pageout(vma);\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE\n\tif (pmd_trans_huge(*pmd)) {\n\t\tpmd_t orig_pmd;\n\t\tunsigned long next = pmd_addr_end(addr, end);\n\n\t\ttlb_change_page_size(tlb, HPAGE_PMD_SIZE);\n\t\tptl = pmd_trans_huge_lock(pmd, vma);\n\t\tif (!ptl)\n\t\t\treturn 0;\n\n\t\torig_pmd = *pmd;\n\t\tif (is_huge_zero_pmd(orig_pmd))\n\t\t\tgoto huge_unlock;\n\n\t\tif (unlikely(!pmd_present(orig_pmd))) {\n\t\t\tVM_BUG_ON(thp_migration_supported() &&\n\t\t\t\t\t!pmd_is_migration_entry(orig_pmd));\n\n... [Diff truncated. Dropped 1455 lines] ...\n\n\tif (len_in && !len)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (start + len < start)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/*\n * madvise_should_skip() - Return if the request is invalid or nothing.\n * @start:\tStart address of madvise-requested address range.\n * @len_in:\tLength of madvise-requested address range.\n * @behavior:\tRequested madvise behavior.\n * @err:\tPointer to store an error code from the check.\n *\n * If the specified behaviour is invalid or nothing would occur, we skip the\n * operation.  This function returns true in the cases, otherwise false.  In\n * the former case we store an error on @err.\n */\nstatic bool madvise_should_skip(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tint behavior, int *err)\n{\n\tif (!is_valid_madvise(start, len_in, behavior)) {\n\t\t*err = -EINVAL;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\tif (start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in) == start) {\n\t\t*err = 0;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_madvise_populate(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_READ:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_WRITE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/*\n * untagged_addr_remote() assumes mmap_lock is already held. On\n * architectures like x86 and RISC-V, tagging is tricky because each\n * mm may have a different tagging mask. However, we might only hold\n * the per-VMA lock (currently only local processes are supported),\n * so untagged_addr is used to avoid the mmap_lock assertion for\n * local processes.\n */\nstatic inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,\n\t\tunsigned long start)\n{\n\treturn current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :\n\t\t\t\t   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_do_behavior(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct blk_plug plug;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\n\tif (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\trange->start = start;\n\t\trange->end = start + len_in;\n\t\treturn madvise_inject_error(madv_behavior);\n\t}\n\n\trange->start = get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start);\n\trange->end = range->start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in);\n\n\tblk_start_plug(&plug);\n\tif (is_madvise_populate(madv_behavior))\n\t\terror = madvise_populate(madv_behavior);\n\telse\n\t\terror = madvise_walk_vmas(madv_behavior);\n\tblk_finish_plug(&plug);\n\treturn error;\n}\n\n/*\n * The madvise(2) system call.\n *\n * Applications can use madvise() to advise the kernel how it should\n * handle paging I/O in this VM area.  The idea is to help the kernel\n * use appropriate read-ahead and caching techniques.  The information\n * provided is advisory only, and can be safely disregarded by the\n * kernel without affecting the correct operation of the application.\n *\n * behavior values:\n *  MADV_NORMAL - the default behavior is to read clusters.  This\n *\t\tresults in some read-ahead and read-behind.\n *  MADV_RANDOM - the system should read the minimum amount of data\n *\t\ton any access, since it is unlikely that the appli-\n *\t\tcation will need more than what it asks for.\n *  MADV_SEQUENTIAL - pages in the given range will probably be accessed\n *\t\tonce, so they can be aggressively read ahead, and\n *\t\tcan be freed soon after they are accessed.\n *  MADV_WILLNEED - the application is notifying the system to read\n *\t\tsome pages ahead.\n *  MADV_DONTNEED - the application is finished with the given range,\n *\t\tso the kernel can free resources associated with it.\n *  MADV_FREE - the application marks pages in the given range as lazy free,\n *\t\twhere actual purges are postponed until memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_REMOVE - the application wants to free up the given range of\n *\t\tpages and associated backing store.\n *  MADV_DONTFORK - omit this area from child's address space when forking:\n *\t\ttypically, to avoid COWing pages pinned by get_user_pages().\n *  MADV_DOFORK - cancel MADV_DONTFORK: no longer omit this area when forking.\n *  MADV_WIPEONFORK - present the child process with zero-filled memory in this\n *              range after a fork.\n *  MADV_KEEPONFORK - undo the effect of MADV_WIPEONFORK\n *  MADV_HWPOISON - trigger memory error handler as if the given memory range\n *\t\twere corrupted by unrecoverable hardware memory failure.\n *  MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE - try to soft-offline the given range of memory.\n *  MADV_MERGEABLE - the application recommends that KSM try to merge pages in\n *\t\tthis area with pages of identical content from other such areas.\n *  MADV_UNMERGEABLE- cancel MADV_MERGEABLE: no longer merge pages with others.\n *  MADV_HUGEPAGE - the application wants to back the given range by transparent\n *\t\thuge pages in the future. Existing pages might be coalesced and\n *\t\tnew pages might be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_NOHUGEPAGE - mark the given range as not worth being backed by\n *\t\ttransparent huge pages so the existing pages will not be\n *\t\tcoalesced into THP and new pages will not be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_COLLAPSE - synchronously coalesce pages into new THP.\n *  MADV_DONTDUMP - the application wants to prevent pages in the given range\n *\t\tfrom being included in its core dump.\n *  MADV_DODUMP - cancel MADV_DONTDUMP: no longer exclude from core dump.\n *  MADV_COLD - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tdeactivate pages in this range so that they can be reclaimed\n *\t\teasily if memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_PAGEOUT - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tpage out the pages in this range immediately.\n *  MADV_POPULATE_READ - populate (prefault) page tables readable by\n *\t\ttriggering read faults if required\n *  MADV_POPULATE_WRITE - populate (prefault) page tables writable by\n *\t\ttriggering write faults if required\n *\n * return values:\n *  zero    - success\n *  -EINVAL - start + len < 0, start is not page-aligned,\n *\t\t\"behavior\" is not a valid value, or application\n *\t\tis attempting to release locked or shared pages,\n *\t\tor the specified address range includes file, Huge TLB,\n *\t\tMAP_SHARED or VMPFNMAP range.\n *  -ENOMEM - a kernel resource was temporarily unavailable.\n *  -EPERM  - memory is sealed.\n */\nint do_madvise(struct mm_struct *mm, unsigned long start, size_t len_in, int behavior)\n{\n\tint error;\n\tstruct mmu_gather tlb;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior madv_behavior = {\n\t\t.mm = mm,\n\t\t.behavior = behavior,\n\t\t.tlb = &tlb,\n\t};\n\n\tif (madvise_should_skip(start, len_in, behavior, &error))\n\t\treturn error;\n\terror = madvise_lock(&madv_behavior);\n\tif (error)\n\t\treturn error;\n\tmadvise_init_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\terror = madvise_do_behavior(start, len_in, &madv_behavior);\n\tmadvise_finish_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\tmadvise_unlock(&madv_behavior);\n\n\treturn error;\n}\n\nSYSCALL_DEFINE3(madvise, unsigned long, start, size_t, len_in, int, behavior)\n{\n\treturn do_madvise(current->mm, start, len_in, behavior);\n}\n\n/* Perform an madvise operation over a vector of addresses and lengths. */\nstatic ssize_t vector_madvise(struct mm_struct *mm, struct iov_iter *iter,\n\t\t\t      int behavior)\n{\n\tssize_t ret = 0;\n\tsize_t total_len;\n\tstruct mmu_gather tlb;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior madv_behavior = {\n\t\t.mm = mm,\n\t\t.behavior = behavior,\n\t\t.tlb = &tlb,\n\t};\n\n\ttotal_len = iov_iter_count(iter);\n\n\tret = madvise_lock(&madv_behavior);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\tmadvise_init_tlb(&madv_behavior);\n\n\twhile (iov_iter_count(iter)) {\n\t\tunsigned long start = (unsigned long)iter_iov_addr(iter);\n\t\tsize_t len_in = iter_iov_len(iter);\n\t\tint error;\n\n\t\tif (madvise_should_skip(start, len_in, behavior, &error))\n\t\t\tret = error;\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tret = madvise_do_behavior(start, len_in, &madv_behavior);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * An madvise operation is attempting to restart the syscall,\n\t\t * but we cannot proceed as it would not be correct to repeat\n\t\t * the operation in aggregate, and would be surprising to the\n\t\t * user.\n\t\t *\n\t\t * We drop and reacquire locks so it is safe to just loop and\n\t\t * try again.     "content": "{"content":"-----------------\n\nOne common purpose of data access monitoring is access-aware system efficiency\noptimizations.  For example,\n\n    paging out memory regions that are not accessed for more than two minutes\n\nor\n\n    using THP for memory regions that are larger than 2 MiB and showing a high\n    access frequency for more than one minute.\n\nOne straightforward approach for such schemes would be profile-guided\noptimizations.  That is, getting data access monitoring results of the\nworkloads or the system using DAMON, finding memory regions of special\ncharacteristics by profiling the monitoring results, and making system\noperation changes for the regions.  The changes could be made by modifying or\nproviding advice to the software (the application and/or the kernel), or\nreconfiguring the hardware.  Both offline and online approaches could be\navailable.\n\nAmong those, providing advice to the kernel at runtime would be flexible and\neffective, and therefore widely be used.   However, implementing such schemes\ncould impose unnecessary redundancy and inefficiency.  The profiling could be\nredundant if the type of interest is common.  Exchanging the information\nincluding monitoring results and operation advice between kernel and user\nspaces could be inefficient.\n\nTo allow users to reduce such redundancy and inefficiencies by offloading the\nworks, DAMON provides a feature called Data Access Monitoring-based Operation\nSchemes (DAMOS).  It lets users specify their desired schemes at a high\nlevel.  For such specifications, DAMON starts monitoring, finds regions having\nthe access pattern of interest, and applies the user-desired operation actions\nto the regions, for every user-specified time interval called\n``apply_interval``.\n\nTo know how user-space can set ``apply_interval`` via :ref:`DAMON sysfs\ninterface <sysfs_interface>`, refer to :ref:`apply_interval_us <sysfs_scheme>`\npart of the documentation.\n\n\n.. _damon_design_damos_action:\n\nOperation Action\n~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n\nThe management action that the users desire to apply to the regions of their\ninterest.  For example, paging out, prioritizing for next reclamation victim\nselection, advising ``khugepaged`` to collapse or split, or doing nothing but\ncollecting statistics of the regions.\n\nThe list of supported actions is defined in DAMOS, but the implementation of\neach action is in the DAMON operations set layer because the implementation\nnormally depends on the monitoring target address space.  For example, the code\nfor paging specific virtual address ranges out would be different from that for\nphysical address ranges.  And the monitoring operations implementation sets are\nnot mandated to support all actions of the list.  Hence, the availability of\nspecific DAMOS action depends on what operations set is selected to be used\ntogether.\n\nThe list of the supported actions, their meaning, and DAMON operations sets\nthat supports each action are as below.\n\n - ``willneed``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_WILLNEED``.\n   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set.\n - ``cold``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_COLD``.\n   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set.\n - ``pageout``: Reclaim the region.\n   Supported by ``vaddr``, ``fvaddr`` and ``paddr`` operations set.\n - ``hugepage``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_HUGEPAGE``.\n   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set. When\n   TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is disabled, the application of the action will just\n   fail.\n - ``nohugepage``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_NOHUGEPAGE``.\n   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set. When\n   TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is disabled, the application of the action will just\n   fail.\n - ``collapse``: Call ``madvise()`` for the region with ``MADV_COLLAPSE``.\n   Supported by ``vaddr`` and ``fvaddr`` operations set. When\n   TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is disabled, the application of the action will just\n   fail.\n - ``lru_prio``: Prioritize the region on its LRU lists.\n   Supported by ``paddr`` operations set.\n - ``lru_deprio``: Deprioritize the region on its LRU lists.\n   Supported by ``paddr`` operations set.\n - ``migrate_hot``: Migrate the regions prioritizing warmer regions.\n   Supported by ``vaddr``, ``fvaddr`` and ``paddr`` operations set.\n - ``migrate_cold``: Migrate the regions prioritizing colder regions.\n   Supported by ``vaddr``, ``fvaddr`` and ``paddr`` operations set.\n - ``stat``: Do nothing but count the statistics.\n   Supported by all operations sets.\n\nApplying the actions except ``stat`` to a region is considered as changing the\nregion's characteristics.  Hence, DAMOS resets the age of regions when any such\nactions are applied to those.\n\nTo know how user-space can set the action via :ref:`DAMON sysfs interface\n<sysfs_interface>`, refer to :ref:`action <sysfs_scheme>` part of the\ndocumentation.\n\n","end_line":500,"start_line":400,"total_lines":886}",

```python
            'lru_deprio': 7,
            'migrate_hot': 8,
            'migrate_cold': 9,
            'stat': 10,
```

```c
	DAMOS_LRU_DEPRIO, // 7
	DAMOS_MIGRATE_HOT, // 8
	DAMOS_MIGRATE_COLD, // 9
	DAMOS_STAT, // 10
	NR_DAMOS_ACTIONS, // 11
};
```

It seems to kick things off by calling `damon_get_mm(target)`, which I'm guessing is responsible for obtaining a reference to the memory management structure, `mm`.  Makes sense, we need to work with the memory maps.

The core operation then appears to be a call to `do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior)`.  No surprises there, that's where the actual madvise action happens. Now, digging deeper, `do_madvise()` calls `madvise_lock()`, which is crucial. This acquires either the `mmap_read_lock` or the `mmap_write_lock`, the choice depending on the `behavior` argument. However, that MADV_COLLAPSE is interesting, since it could use the `mmap_read_lock`. I need to verify that!

Okay, time to check the source code for `madvise_lock()` in `mm/madvise.c`. Gotta get a better grasp on the locking behavior, especially how it's handled for different advice types.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Lock Behavior of MADV_COLLAPSE**

Okay, so I'm looking into the locking behavior around `MADV_COLLAPSE`. My initial understanding was that `get_lock_mode` defaults to `MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK` when handling `MADV_COLLAPSE`, at least based on my initial assumptions.  But, wait, looking at the code in `mm/madvise.c`, there's no explicit case for `MADV_COLLAPSE` within `get_lock_mode`. This means, as I suspected, it falls into the `default` case, leading to `MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK` being returned. I should probably double-check that to be certain.

Let me just quickly confirm my suspicions about the code:

```c
static enum madvise_lock_mode get_lock_mode(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)
{
	if (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior))
		return MADVISE_NO_LOCK;

	switch (madv_behavior->behavior) {
	case MADV_REMOVE:
	case MADV_WILLNEED:
	case MADV_COLD:
	case MADV_PAGEOUT:
	case MADV_POPULATE_READ:
...
	case MADV_FREE:
		return MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK;
	default:
		return MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK;
	}
}
```

Yep, confirmed, the default case indeed returns `MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK`.  So, that means `mmap_write_lock_killable(mm)` should be taken.

Now, hold on a second.  This is where it gets interesting. I need to refresh my memory and check out `madvise_collapse_errno()` and `madvise_collapse()` in `mm/khugepaged.c`.

```c
int madvise_collapse(struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long start,
		     unsigned long end, bool *lock_dropped)
{
	...
out_maybelock:
	/* Caller expects us to hold mmap_lock on return */
	if (*lock_dropped)
		mmap_read_lock(mm);
...
```

Ah-ha!  `madvise_collapse` explicitly *expects* that if the `lock_dropped` flag is set, it reacquires the `mmap_read_lock(mm)`. This is key.

But now, something is nagging at me. If `get_lock_mode` *always* returns `MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK` for `MADV_COLLAPSE` (which the check above confirms it does), then `madvise_collapse` reacquiring `mmap_read_lock` *after potentially dropping the lock* would result in a lock downgrade! That just doesn't seem right. That doesn't seem like the correct behavior. What lock is being dropped? And is it correct?

My next step is to figure out exactly what lock `madvise_collapse` is dropping. And I also need to verify whether the returned lock is actually `MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK` or possibly something else like `MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK`. That's where I need to focus my investigation.

Okay, so I'm looking at how `MADV_COLLAPSE` is handled in the kernel, specifically concerning `-ERESTARTNOINTR`. My initial thought was, "Hey, `madvise_do_behavior` returns `-ERESTARTNOINTR` sometimes, but how is that handled in `do_madvise()`?".

Turns out, `do_madvise()` just naively returns whatever `madvise_do_behavior` returns. It goes something like this: It locks, initializes TLB, calls `madvise_do_behavior`, finishes TLB handling, unlocks, and returns. If `madvise_do_behavior` returns `-ERESTARTNOINTR`, the syscall just returns that error. I thought this was weird, because `-ERESTARTNOINTR` should usually trigger a restart in the syscall machinery.

Then I recalled that `damos_madvise`, which I'm dealing with, returns `0` upon failure, which would just skip over the call. But then I remember `MADV_COLLAPSE` doesn't normally return `-ERESTARTNOINTR`. So, is it `madvise_collapse`?

Aha! Now I remembered the comment in `vector_madvise()` which states that `-ERESTARTNOINTR` might arise with behaviors like `MADV_GUARD_INSTALL`. It has to do with things disrupted by `fatal_signal_pending()`.

So then the question becomes, does `madvise_collapse` return `-ERESTARTNOINTR`? No, it looks like `madvise_collapse_errno` translates errors into common errors like `ENOMEM`, `EAGAIN`, or `EBUSY`.

But, wait! The crucial part is about the lock. Does `madvise_collapse` drop the lock? I see that `madvise_collapse` does return an error and also modifies `*lock_dropped`. The interesting part is that `madvise_collapse` is directly called from `madvise_vma_behavior` and `madvise_walk_vmas`. I think I need to look into `madvise_walk_vmas` now.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Thread Safety of MADV_COLLAPSE with DAMON**

Okay, so `do_madvise` seems to be working as expected for `MADV_COLLAPSE`. Specifically, `damos_madvise()` does: `applied = do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior) ? 0 : len;`. Right, it ignores errors; a return of `0` indicates something went wrong. But that brings up the question of whether `MADV_COLLAPSE` is synchronous or asynchronous. Ah, it's synchronous; `MADV_COLLAPSE` explicitly coalesces pages into new THPs in a blocking manner.

Now, the crucial part: the memory management lock. `damos_madvise` gets `mm = damon_get_mm(target)` and then calls `do_madvise`. `do_madvise` then takes `mmap_read_lock(mm)` for `MADV_COLLAPSE`. Is there a potential context issue here? DAMON is running from a kthread, `kdamond`. So, the `current` task is `kdamond`, which will be different from the process we're madvising.

`do_madvise` correctly uses `madv_behavior.mm = mm`. It doesn't appear to rely on `current->mm` directly, BUT... I need to be absolutely sure. I need to dive into `do_madvise` and its called functions: `madvise_collapse` and its callees. Are any of those functions, or their callees, inadvertently using `current->mm`?

I'll search within `madvise_collapse`, and functions it calls such as `collapse_scan_pmd` and `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp`, and the `khugepaged.c` file in general. Gotta eliminate any possibility of a race condition or incorrect MM context usage. This is a critical point to ensure DAMON's madvise operation doesn't corrupt anything.

    "thought": "**DAMOS and MADV_COLLAPSE: A Safety and Completeness Check**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about the safety of using `current->mm` when calling `do_madvise` and `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp`. Since these functions don't actually rely on `current->mm` being the same as `vma->vm_mm`, running them from a different task, like a `kdamond` thread, and using the target memory map (`mm`) is perfectly fine.

Then I double-checked the error handling in `damos_madvise()`.  The code's returning the number of bytes applied by checking the return of `do_madvise()`: `applied = do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior) ? 0 : len;`. If `do_madvise` fails, it returns 0. I was concerned about  `-ERESTARTNOINTR`, but it turns out `MADV_COLLAPSE` does not return this error. I confirmed this by checking `madvise_collapse`, which returns codes like `-ENOMEM`, `-EAGAIN`, `-EBUSY`, and `-EINVAL`.  So `damos_madvise` will behave correctly.

Next, I scrutinized the locking. The `MADV_COLLAPSE` operation does something interesting: it *drops* and potentially *re-acquires* the `mmap_read_lock`. The crucial part here is the `lock_dropped` flag. If `madvise_collapse` drops the lock and then re-acquires it, the VMA might have been freed or split in the meantime! The `lock_dropped` flag signals that the VMA might no longer be valid.  `madvise_walk_vmas` handles this by invalidating the VMA pointer and restarting the lookup from `range->end`, which is safe. So the locking is correct.

I also thought about the `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` implementation returning `applied` correctly. If `madvise_collapse` returns `0`, DAMON will know that the collapse succeeded. However, since the function returns `0` if *any* page fails, DAMON might not be able to track partial successes. But that's a common characteristic with other DAMOS madvise actions.

Finally, I checked if the new `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` action had any unintended side effects. I confirmed it was correctly inserted in the `enum damos_action` and added to `damos_sysfs_action_names`. There don't seem to be any array sizing issues or indexing problems with the new action. It looks safe.

Okay, so I'm diving into this `madvise_collapse` implementation in DAMON, and here's what I'm thinking:

First, confirmed – `madvise_collapse` is correctly defined, and it returns `-EINVAL` when `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is disabled. That's expected behavior, as the documentation in `damon/design.rst` explicitly mentions this failure mode. So far, so good. The return code propagates up, eventually returning 0 from `damos_madvise`, which makes sense.

Next, a quick check of another file that was modified. It looks like `tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py` was updated. It defines a mapping of strings to numbers, and the key `collapse` has been added. There's also a preexisting typo of `nohugeapge` which is odd, but doesn't introduce any new regressions.

Now, a critical thought: how does this interact with cgroups and namespaces? Does `MADV_COLLAPSE` bypass any resource limits? Digging into `madvise_collapse`, I see it calls functions like `collapse_pte_mapped_thp` and `collapse_single_pmd`. The crucial point is memory accounting. I need to understand where the THP allocations are charged. Normally, `khugepaged` will charge memory to the memcg of the process, but `damos_madvise` runs in the context of the `kdamond` thread. I need to be sure the allocations are charged appropriately.

Looking at `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp`, I see `vma_alloc_folio` is used with `GFP_TRANSHUGE`.  That uses `mem_cgroup_charge` which in turn relies on `mm`. And yes, `mem_cgroup_charge` correctly uses the `mm`! Specifically, it uses `get_mem_cgroup_from_mm`, meaning it *will* charge to the target `mm`, not `kdamond`.     "thought": "**DAMON MADV_COLLAPSE Dive: A Deep-Dive**

Okay, so I'm digging into this `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` addition in DAMON, and it seems like it's inheriting a pattern from the existing `madvise`-based actions. Specifically, `damon_va_apply_scheme` delegates to `damos_madvise` for the *madvise* actions:  `DAMOS_WILLNEED`, `DAMOS_COLD`, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT`, etc. and now `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`.  The code simply ignores filters when applying these madvise actions.  I'm noting that DAMON's core should check the core-level filters though, so that's something to remember.

My initial thought was that filters might not be supported for `madvise` actions. The documentation mentions a new `damos_filter_type` which seems like something important, but I can't find it in the patch I received, that's odd, and something I need to look closer at in the original commit. However, the documentation for `enum damos_action` in `include/linux/damon.h` lists `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`.

`DAMOS_PAGEOUT` uses `damos_madvise` which, in turn, uses `MADV_PAGEOUT`. This pattern doesn't apply filters. The core question is whether `damos_ops_has_filter(s)` returns true.  If it does, I want to confirm `damos_madvise` either fails or ignores it, which it ignores, which is the current behavior for all `MADV_*` based actions in DAMON. It doesn't seem to be introducing a new bug here. It's simply following existing practice.

Next, I need to check `do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior)`. It expects `len` as the length, which is `PAGE_ALIGN(damon_sz_region(r))`. Potential for an infinite loop with `do_madvise` iterating VMAs is a concern.  It drops and reacquires the lock, and restarts. However, this is only in `vector_madvise` and deals with `-ERESTARTNOINTR`.  The more general `do_madvise` just returns the value from `madvise_do_behavior`.

A red flag: What if `madvise_do_behavior` returns `-ERESTARTNOINTR` *within* `do_madvise()`? It just returns `-ERESTARTNOINTR`. It doesn't retry! However, the `madvise` syscall would be restarted, but DAMON calls `do_madvise` directly from the kernel! If `do_madvise` returns `-ERESTARTNOINTR`, `damos_madvise` just gets 0 (`applied = do_madvise(...) ? 0 : len`). Is this a bug?  It means DAMON *skips* the region upon restart, but is this acceptable? After all, it is a best-effort system. Luckily, `madvise_collapse` doesn't return `-ERESTARTNOINTR`. It handles lock drops internally, which seems good. It modifies `madv_behavior->lock_dropped` and lets `madvise_walk_vmas` refind the VMA.

Now, let's look at `madvise_collapse` in `mm/khugepaged.c`. It re-acquires `mmap_read_lock(mm)` if the lock was dropped, upon return. Then `madvise_vma_behavior` returns the error code to `madvise_walk_vmas`. If the return code is 0 (success), `madvise_walk_vmas` sees `madv_behavior->lock_dropped == true`, clears the `prev` and `vma` pointers, resets `lock_dropped` to false, then `find_vma` to continue, which seems correct.  I'm now sure that `madvise_collapse` re-acquires `mmap_read_lock`. This is important, as `madvise_walk_vmas` *expects* the lock to be held.  Why? because the lock was dropped and has been re-acquired.

The `madvise_walk_vmas` checks `lock_dropped`: if set, then the lock WAS dropped, and MUST be held. It sets `vma = NULL` to force `find_vma`. This all seems correct.

Looking deeper, the `Damos` class in `sysfs.py` initializes `self.action`. It appears `action_val` maps the string version of the action to some integer value. Okay, so if the `scheme.action` is the string 'collapse' and the `dump['action']` is 5, then it is correct.

MADV_HUGEPAGE:\n\tcase MADV_NOHUGEPAGE:\n\tcase MADV_COLLAPSE:\n#endif\n\tcase MADV_DONTDUMP:\n\tcase MADV_DODUMP:\n\tcase MADV_WIPEONFORK:\n\tcase MADV_KEEPONFORK:\n\tcase MADV_GUARD_INSTALL:\n\tcase MADV_GUARD_REMOVE:\n#ifdef CONFIG_MEMORY_FAILURE\n\tcase MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE:\n\tcase MADV_HWPOISON:\n#endif\n\t\treturn true;\n\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/* Can we invoke process_madvise() on a remote mm for the specified behavior? */\nstatic bool process_madvise_remote_valid(int behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_COLD:\n\tcase MADV_PAGEOUT:\n\tcase MADV_WILLNEED:\n\tcase MADV_COLLAPSE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/* Does this operation invoke anon_vma_prepare()? */\nstatic bool prepares_anon_vma(int behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_GUARD_INSTALL:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/*\n * We have acquired a VMA read lock, is the VMA valid to be madvise'd under VMA\n * read lock only now we have a VMA to examine?\n */\nstatic bool is_vma_lock_sufficient(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\t/* Must span only a single VMA.*/\n\tif (madv_behavior->range.end > vma->vm_end)\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/* Remote processes unsupported. */\n\tif (current->mm != vma->vm_mm)\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/* Userfaultfd unsupported. */\n\tif (userfaultfd_armed(vma))\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/*\n\t * anon_vma_prepare() explicitly requires an mmap lock for\n\t * serialisation, so we cannot use a VMA lock in this case.\n\t *\n\t * Note we might race with anon_vma being set, however this makes this\n\t * check overly paranoid which is safe.\n\t */\n\tif (vma_is_anonymous(vma) &&\n\t    prepares_anon_vma(madv_behavior->behavior) && !vma->anon_vma)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Try to acquire a VMA read lock if possible.\n *\n * We only support this lock over a single VMA, which the input range must\n * span either partially or fully.\n *\n * This function always returns with an appropriate lock held. If a VMA read\n * lock could be acquired, we return true and set madv_behavior state\n * accordingly.\n *\n * If a VMA read lock could not be acquired, we return false and expect caller to\n * fallback to mmap lock behaviour.\n */\nstatic bool try_vma_read_lock(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma;\n\n\tvma = lock_vma_under_rcu(mm, madv_behavior->range.start);\n\tif (!vma)\n\t\tgoto take_mmap_read_lock;\n\n\tif (!is_vma_lock_sufficient(vma, madv_behavior)) {\n\t\tvma_end_read(vma);\n\t\tgoto take_mmap_read_lock;\n\t}\n\n\tmadv_behavior->vma = vma;\n\treturn true;\n\ntake_mmap_read_lock:\n\tmmap_read_lock(mm);\n\tmadv_behavior->lock_mode = MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK;\n\treturn false;\n}\n\n/*\n * Walk the vmas in range [start,end), and call the madvise_vma_behavior\n * function on each one.  The function will get start and end parameters that\n * cover the overlap between the current vma and the original range.  Any\n * unmapped regions in the original range will result in this function returning\n * -ENOMEM while still calling the madvise_vma_behavior function on all of the\n * existing vmas in the range.  Must be called with the mmap_lock held for\n * reading or writing.\n */\nstatic\nint madvise_walk_vmas(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\t/* range is updated to span each VMA, so store end of entire range. */\n\tunsigned long last_end = range->end;\n\tint unmapped_error = 0;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev, *vma;\n\n\t/*\n\t * If VMA read lock is supported, apply madvise to a single VMA\n\t * tentatively, avoiding walking VMAs.\n\t */\n\tif (madv_behavior->lock_mode == MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK &&\n\t    try_vma_read_lock(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\terror = madvise_vma_behavior(madv_behavior);\n\t\tvma_end_read(madv_behavior->vma);\n\t\treturn error;\n\t}\n\n\tvma = find_vma_prev(mm, range->start, &prev);\n\tif (vma && range->start > vma->vm_start)\n\t\tprev = vma;\n\n\tfor (;;) {\n\t\t/* Still start < end. */\n\t\tif (!vma)\n\t\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t\t/* Here start < (last_end|vma->vm_end). */\n\t\tif (range->start < vma->vm_start) {\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * This indicates a gap between VMAs in the input\n\t\t\t * range. This does not cause the operation to abort,\n\t\t\t * rather we simply return -ENOMEM to indicate that this\n\t\t\t * has happened, but carry on.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tunmapped_error = -ENOMEM;\n\t\t\trange->start = vma->vm_start;\n\t\t\tif (range->start >= last_end)\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t/* Here vma->vm_start <= range->start < (last_end|vma->vm_end) */\n\t\trange->end = min(vma->vm_end, last_end);\n\n\t\t/* Here vma->vm_start <= range->start < range->end <= (last_end|vma->vm_end). */\n\t\tmadv_behavior->prev = prev;\n\t\tmadv_behavior->vma = vma;\n\t\terror = madvise_vma_behavior(madv_behavior);\n\t\tif (error)\n\t\t\treturn error;\n\t\tif (madv_behavior->lock_dropped) {\n\t\t\t/* We dropped the mmap lock, we can't ref the VMA. */\n\t\t\tprev = NULL;\n\t\t\tvma = NULL;\n\t\t\tmadv_behavior->lock_dropped = false;\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tvma = madv_behavior->vma;\n\t\t\tprev = vma;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (vma && range->end < vma->vm_end)\n\t\t\trange->end = vma->vm_end;\n\t\tif (range->end >= last_end)\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\tvma = find_vma(mm, vma ? vma->vm_end : range->end);\n\t\trange->start = range->end;\n\t}\n\n\treturn unmapped_error;\n}\n\n/*\n * Any behaviour which results in changes to the vma->vm_flags needs to\n * take mmap_lock for writing. Others, which simply traverse vmas, need\n * to only take it for reading.\n */\nstatic enum madvise_lock_mode get_lock_mode(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tif (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior))\n\t\treturn MADVISE_NO_LOCK;\n\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_REMOVE:\n\tcase MADV_WILLNEED:\n\tcase MADV_COLD:\n\tcase MADV_PAGEOUT:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_READ:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_WRITE:\n\tcase MADV_COLLAPSE:\n\t\treturn MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK;\n\tcase MADV_GUARD_INSTALL:\n\tcase MADV_GUARD_REMOVE:\n\tcase MADV_DONTNEED:\n\tcase MADV_DONTNEED_LOCKED:\n\tcase MADV_FREE:\n\t\treturn MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK;\n\t}\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_lock(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\tenum madvise_lock_mode lock_mode = get_lock_mode(madv_behavior);\n\n\tswitch (lock_mode) {\n\tcase MADVISE_NO_LOCK:\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK:\n\t\tif (mmap_write_lock_killable(mm))\n\t\t\treturn -EINTR;\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK:\n\t\tmmap_read_lock(mm);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK:\n\t\t/* We will acquire the lock per-VMA in madvise_walk_vmas(). */\n\t\tbreak;\n\t}\n\n\tmadv_behavior->lock_mode = lock_mode;\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic void madvise_unlock(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = madv_behavior->mm;\n\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->lock_mode) {\n\tcase  MADVISE_NO_LOCK:\n\t\treturn;\n\tcase MADVISE_MMAP_WRITE_LOCK:\n\t\tmmap_write_unlock(mm);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK:\n\t\tmmap_read_unlock(mm);\n\t\tbreak;\n\tcase MADVISE_VMA_READ_LOCK:\n\t\t/* We will drop the lock per-VMA in madvise_walk_vmas(). */\n\t\tbreak;\n\t}\n\n\tmadv_behavior->lock_mode = MADVISE_NO_LOCK;\n}\n\nstatic bool madvise_batch_tlb_flush(int behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_DONTNEED:\n\tcase MADV_DONTNEED_LOCKED:\n\tcase MADV_FREE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\nstatic void madvise_init_tlb(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tif (madvise_batch_tlb_flush(madv_behavior->behavior))\n\t\ttlb_gather_mmu(madv_behavior->tlb, madv_behavior->mm);\n}\n\nstatic void madvise_finish_tlb(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tif (madvise_batch_tlb_flush(madv_behavior->behavior))\n\t\ttlb_finish_mmu(madv_behavior->tlb);\n}\n\nstatic bool is_valid_madvise(unsigned long start, size_t len_in, int behavior)\n{\n\tsize_t len;\n\n\tif (!madvise_behavior_valid(behavior))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (!PAGE_ALIGNED(start))\n\t\treturn false;\n\tlen = PAGE_ALIGN(len_in);\n\n\t/* Check to see whether len was rounded up from small -ve to zero */\n\tif (len_in && !len)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (start + len < start)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/*\n * madvise_should_skip() - Return if the request is invalid or nothing.\n * @start:\tStart address of madvise-requested address range.\n * @len_in:\tLength of madvise-requested address range.\n * @behavior:\tRequested madvise behavior.\n * @err:\tPointer to store an error code from the check.\n *\n * If the specified behaviour is invalid or nothing would occur, we skip the\n * operation.  This function returns true in the cases, otherwise false.  In\n * the former case we store an error on @err.\n */\nstatic bool madvise_should_skip(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tint behavior, int *err)\n{\n\tif (!is_valid_madvise(start, len_in, behavior)) {\n\t\t*err = -EINVAL;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\tif (start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in) == start) {\n\t\t*err = 0;\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_madvise_populate(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tswitch (madv_behavior->behavior) {\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_READ:\n\tcase MADV_POPULATE_WRITE:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\n/*\n * untagged_addr_remote() assumes mmap_lock is already held. On\n * architectures like x86 and RISC-V, tagging is tricky because each\n * mm may have a different tagging mask. However, we might only hold\n * the per-VMA lock (currently only local processes are supported),\n * so untagged_addr is used to avoid the mmap_lock assertion for\n * local processes.\n */\nstatic inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,\n\t\tunsigned long start)\n{\n\treturn current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :\n\t\t\t\t   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);\n}\n\nstatic int madvise_do_behavior(unsigned long start, size_t len_in,\n\t\tstruct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)\n{\n\tstruct blk_plug plug;\n\tint error;\n\tstruct madvise_behavior_range *range = &madv_behavior->range;\n\n\tif (is_memory_failure(madv_behavior)) {\n\t\trange->start = start;\n\t\trange->end = start + len_in;\n\t\treturn madvise_inject_error(madv_behavior);\n\t}\n\n\trange->start = get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start);\n\trange->end = range->start + PAGE_ALIGN(len_in);\n\n\tblk_start_plug(&plug);\n\tif (is_madvise_populate(madv_behavior))\n\t\terror = madvise_populate(madv_behavior);\n\telse\n\t\terror = madvise_walk_vmas(madv_behavior);\n\tblk_finish_plug(&plug);\n\treturn error;\n}\n\n/*\n * The madvise(2) system call.\n *\n * Applications can use madvise() to advise the kernel how it should\n * handle paging I/O in this VM area.  The idea is to help the kernel\n * use appropriate read-ahead and caching techniques.  The information\n * provided is advisory only, and can be safely disregarded by the\n * kernel without affecting the correct operation of the application.\n *\n * behavior values:\n *  MADV_NORMAL - the default behavior is to read clusters.  This\n *\t\tresults in some read-ahead and read-behind.\n *  MADV_RANDOM - the system should read the minimum amount of data\n *\t\ton any access, since it is unlikely that the appli-\n *\t\tcation will need more than what it asks for.\n *  MADV_SEQUENTIAL - pages in the given range will probably be accessed\n *\t\tonce, so they can be aggressively read ahead, and\n *\t\tcan be freed soon after they are accessed.\n *  MADV_WILLNEED - the application is notifying the system to read\n *\t\tsome pages ahead.\n *  MADV_DONTNEED - the application is finished with the given range,\n *\t\tso the kernel can free resources associated with it.\n *  MADV_FREE - the application marks pages in the given range as lazy free,\n *\t\twhere actual purges are postponed until memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_REMOVE - the application wants to free up the given range of\n *\t\tpages and associated backing store.\n *  MADV_DONTFORK - omit this area from child's address space when forking:\n *\t\ttypically, to avoid COWing pages pinned by get_user_pages().\n *  MADV_DOFORK - cancel MADV_DONTFORK: no longer omit this area when forking.\n *  MADV_WIPEONFORK - present the child process with zero-filled memory in this\n *              range after a fork.\n *  MADV_KEEPONFORK - undo the effect of MADV_WIPEONFORK\n *  MADV_HWPOISON - trigger memory error handler as if the given memory range\n *\t\twere corrupted by unrecoverable hardware memory failure.\n *  MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE - try to soft-offline the given range of memory.\n *  MADV_MERGEABLE - the application recommends that KSM try to merge pages in\n *\t\tthis area with pages of identical content from other such areas.\n *  MADV_UNMERGEABLE- cancel MADV_MERGEABLE: no longer merge pages with others.\n *  MADV_HUGEPAGE - the application wants to back the given range by transparent\n *\t\thuge pages in the future. Existing pages might be coalesced and\n *\t\tnew pages might be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_NOHUGEPAGE - mark the given range as not worth being backed by\n *\t\ttransparent huge pages so the existing pages will not be\n *\t\tcoalesced into THP and new pages will not be allocated as THP.\n *  MADV_COLLAPSE - synchronously coalesce pages into new THP.\n *  MADV_DONTDUMP - the application wants to prevent pages in the given range\n *\t\tfrom being included in its core dump.\n *  MADV_DODUMP - cancel MADV_DONTDUMP: no longer exclude from core dump.\n *  MADV_COLD - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tdeactivate pages in this range so that they can be reclaimed\n *\t\teasily if memory pressure happens.\n *  MADV_PAGEOUT - the application is not expected to use this memory soon,\n *\t\tpage out the pages in this range immediately.\n *  MADV_POPULATE_READ - populate (prefault) page tables readable by\n *\t\ttriggering read faults if required\n *  MADV_POPULATE_WRITE - populate (prefault) page tables writable by\n *\t\ttriggering write faults if required\n *\n * return values:\n *  zero    - success\n *  -EINVAL - start + len < 0, start is not page-aligned,\n *\t\t\"behavior\" is not a valid value, or application\n *\t\tis attempting to release locked or shared pages,\n *\t\tor the specified address range includes file, Huge TLB,\n *\t\tMAP_SHARED or VMPFNMAP range.\n *  -ENOMEM - addresses in the specified range are not currently\n *\t\tmapped, or are outside the AS of the process.\n *  -EIO    - an I/O error occurred while paging in data.\n *  -EBADF  - map exists, but area maps something that isn't a file.\n *  -EAGAIN -     "thought": "**Analyzing MADV_COLLAPSE and DAMON Integration**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was about how the `mmap_lock` is handled during `MADV_COLLAPSE` operations, specifically whether there was a potential race condition or lock inconsistency. It seems like the core issue revolves around `collapse_single_pmd` and its interaction with `mmap_read_lock` and `mmap_read_unlock`. If `collapse_single_pmd` dropped the lock, and returned SCAN_PTE_MAPPED_HUGEPAGE, then the lock would have been re-acquired. The `*lock_dropped` flag is a signal.

Now, looking deeper at `madvise_collapse`, it re-acquires the lock if `*lock_dropped` is true but doesn't reset it. This initially struck me as a potential problem because it leaves the flag set for the caller, *even if* the lock is held. However, after tracing the call stack through `madvise_vma_behavior` and `madvise_walk_vmas`, it appears this is intentional and, critically, correct. The `madv_behavior->lock_dropped` flag is used as an indication that the VMA pointer may be stale, not that the lock is actually dropped at the time of return from `madvise_vma_behavior()`. In the case of success it's safe because `madvise_walk_vmas` re-looks up the VMA. In the case of failure, `madvise_unlock` will unlock the lock.

So, it's safe to conclude that `MADV_COLLAPSE` itself is implemented correctly regarding lock management.

Now, let's shift our focus to DAMON's integration with `MADV_COLLAPSE`. DAMON simply calls `do_madvise` with `MADV_COLLAPSE`. This appears straightforward. However, it's essential to scrutinize `damos_madvise()`'s return value and the broader DAMON data structures to ensure there are no subtle inconsistencies. Particularly, there is a mismatch when `do_madvise` fails, it returns 0 bytes applied.

I need to confirm there is not other locations where this enum is used.

The `damon_sysfs_scheme_alloc` also sets `scheme->action = action;`, which implies that the relationship between the `damos_action` enum and the values passed to `do_madvise` must be consistent. And finally, I need to check the selftest and the documentation.

A quick check of the DAMON selftest script (`sysfs.py`) revealed a minor typo (`nohugeapge` vs `nohugepage`) which could indicate a general lack of rigor. It doesn't affect the C code and is not critical.

Also, I checked the sysfs documentation. It could have been out of sync with the code.

Therefore, everything seems to be in order for `MADV_COLLAPSE` and DAMON integration.

    "thought": "**DAMOS Patch Analysis: MADV_COLLAPSE Integration**

Okay, let's break this down. First, the documentation refers to `design doc <damon_design_damos_action>`, and the patch updates `Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst` to include `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`. That's good; the documentation side of things seems complete.

Now, the core of the issue: is there any problem with `madvise_collapse` returning `-EINVAL` for anonymous memory? I initially thought there might be an issue. However, `madvise_collapse` *does* support anonymous memory via `collapse_scan_pmd`. The code confirms this, with the check for `vma_is_anonymous(vma)`. The use of `thp_vma_allowable_order()` also makes me confident about this.

A potential concern arose regarding `lru_add_drain_all()` being called before the iteration. `lru_add_drain_all()` could sleep. But, `damos_madvise` doesn't hold any spinlocks, and it's running in a kernel thread, so sleeping is perfectly fine. `do_madvise` acquires the mmap lock, which also allows sleeping. That's fine.

The `target_nid` parameter in `madvise_collapse` doesn't seem to be used by the patch because `damos_madvise` ignores it, so that's not an issue.

Now, the elephant in the room: `sz_filter_passed`. In `damon_va_apply_scheme`, `sz_filter_passed` is used. However, for `damos_madvise`, it just returns `applied`. `sz_filter_passed` is used by `damos_va_stat` and `damos_va_migrate` to update the filter value. The other actions like `DAMOS_WILLNEED`, `DAMOS_COLD`, `DAMOS_PAGEOUT`, `DAMOS_HUGEPAGE`, `DAMOS_NOHUGEPAGE` and `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` don't update `sz_filter_passed` and don't support `sz_filter_passed` filtering inside `damos_madvise`. This means the filtering is bypassed. The question is: is this by design or a bug? In `damon_va_apply_scheme`, `sz_filter_passed` is an output pointer. If `damos_ops_has_filter(scheme)` is true, `damos_madvise` does *not* check it. Based on the code, filtering is only applied at the region level for the core-layer filters, but for ops-layer filters, only `DAMOS_STAT` and `DAMOS_MIGRATE_*` check them. This is an existing limitation. I need to make sure this isn't a regression, but it appears to be an existing design choice. This isn't specific to this patch, but worth noting.

If `MADV_COLLAPSE` returns `0` bytes applied on failure, is this because of a temporary failure? In this case, 0 bytes applied is fine. DAMON will just record this value.

Finally, checking the `tools/testing/selftests/damon/sysfs.py` script. The patch adds `'collapse': 5,`. The enum values in `include/linux/damon.h` are confirmed: `WILLNEED = 0`, `COLD = 1`, `PAGEOUT = 2`, `HUGEPAGE = 3`, `NOHUGEPAGE = 4`, `COLLAPSE = 5`, and the `sysfs.py` is correct. The `action_val` dictionary in the python script is also verified, and it looks correct. The old code is verified to confirm.

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at `madvise_collapse` in `mm/khugepaged.c`. It looks like it drops the lock with `*lock_dropped = true`.  I need to confirm that. Let's see the signature:

```c
int madvise_collapse(struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long start,
		     unsigned long end, bool *lock_dropped)
```

Right, it's there in `mm/madvise.c` too:

```c
static int madvise_vma_behavior(struct madvise_behavior *madv_behavior)
...

		return madvise_collapse(vma, range->start, range->end,
			&madv_behavior->lock_dropped);
```

Okay, now it's coming into focus with DAMON. Inside `damon_va_apply_scheme()`, we see that `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` maps to `MADV_COLLAPSE`.  `damos_madvise()` calls `do_madvise()`.

```c

		struct damon_region *r, int behavior)
{
	struct mm_struct *mm;
...
	mm = damon_get_mm(target);
	if (!mm)
		return 0;

	applied = do_madvise(mm, start, len, behavior) ? 0 : len;
	mmput(mm);

	return applied;
}
```

Wait, `do_madvise()` seems to expect a syscall context, right?  No, actually, it doesn't *require* a syscall context directly. However, the use of `get_untagged_addr(madv_behavior->mm, start)` within `madvise_do_behavior` is interesting.

```c
static inline unsigned long get_untagged_addr(struct mm_struct *mm,
		unsigned long start)
{
	return current->mm == mm ? untagged_addr(start) :
				   untagged_addr_remote(mm, start);
}
```

Okay, so in `kdamond`, `current->mm` is probably NULL, because it's a kernel thread.  But `mm` is the target process's `mm`. So it calls `untagged_addr_remote(mm, start)`. That seems alright, I think.

But what about the lock dropping in `madvise_collapse()`?

In `madvise_collapse()`, `mm` is `vma->vm_mm`. It drops the `mmap_read_lock(mm)` and then reacquires it.

But `madvise_walk_vmas()` uses:

```c
			/* We dropped the mmap lock, we can't ref the VMA. */
			prev = NULL;
			vma = NULL;
			madv_behavior->lock_dropped = false;
		}
```

Yes, and then `madvise_collapse` drops it.

Then, there's a comment in `madvise_collapse` that says:

```c
	/* Caller expects us to hold mmap_lock on return */
	if (*lock_dropped)
		mmap_read_lock(mm);
```

So it drops the lock, but *re-acquires* it before returning. Good.

`do_madvise()` *doesn't* check capabilities. However, `damos_madvise()` runs under `kdamond`, and `kdamond` is started by root, so it *has* capabilities.

Okay, let me walk through this. My initial thought was that `madvise_behavior->lock_dropped` was a clear signal – if true, `madvise_walk_vmas` would invalidate the VMA pointers, which seemed correct. The design's intent, as I understood it, was that if the mmap_lock was dropped and reacquired, the VMA might be stale, so re-lookup was necessary.  `madvise_walk_vmas` seemed to be *expecting* that the lock was held on return from `madvise_vma_behavior`. Since it doesn't re-acquire the lock itself, it just clears the VMA pointers if it knows the lock was dropped. So, if `madvise_collapse` reacquires the lock, it seemed correct to do that, and leave `*lock_dropped = true`.

However, now I see a potential problem.  `madvise_collapse` *sometimes* leaves `*lock_dropped = false` on return. If it loops around, it resets `*lock_dropped = false`! So, if the lock is dropped in iteration 1, reacquired in iteration 2, `*lock_dropped` is set to false, and then the remaining iterations are lock-held, the function returns with a false flag, meaning `madvise_walk_vmas` won't invalidate the VMA pointers!

But is the original VMA valid after a lock drop? During the unlock and re-lock, another thread could split, merge, or unmap the VMA!  This is where things get interesting. Inside `madvise_collapse`,  `hugepage_vma_revalidate` updates a local `vma` variable, but it does NOT update the `madv_behavior->vma` pointer, meaning it remains pointing to the original VMA.  I confirm that `madvise_vma_behavior` only passes the `vma` pointer by value to `madvise_collapse`, not by reference!

Therefore, if `madvise_collapse` drops the lock, reacquires it, and updates *its* local `vma`, but returns with `*lock_dropped = false`,  `madvise_walk_vmas` will erroneously use the original, potentially stale VMA.  This is because  `madvise_walk_vmas` assigns `vma = madv_behavior->vma;`. It then accesses `vma->vm_end`!

Let's trace this flow: `madvise_collapse` is called with `*lock_dropped = false`. In iteration 1, `*lock_dropped` is false, it drops the lock, sets `*lock_dropped = true`.  In iteration 2, `*lock_dropped` is true; it reacquires the lock, sets `*lock_dropped = false`, and `hugepage_vma_revalidate` updates the local `vma`.  The following iterations succeed without dropping the lock. Then returns with `*lock_dropped = false`.  In `madvise_walk_vmas`, the original, *potentially freed* `vma` pointer is used!  The `range->end < vma->vm_end` check then dereferences the original, potentially stale `vma`.  This is a definite use-after-free vulnerability!

    "thought": "**Deep Dive into `madvise_collapse` and Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerability**

Okay, here's my assessment after a thorough examination of the code, specifically focusing on the interaction between `madvise_collapse`, `madvise_walk_vmas`, and the handling of the mmap lock. My concern centers around a potential use-after-free vulnerability, triggered when `madvise_collapse` drops and re-acquires the mmap lock.

Initially, my thought was that `hugepage_vma_revalidate` might be the source of the issue because it updates a local `vma` variable within `madvise_collapse`, but *never* updates the corresponding `madv_behavior->vma`. However, it’s not the assignment itself, but the *implicit* assumption carried by the `madvise_walk_vmas` about the lock state, that is causing the problem.

The crux of the matter lies in how `madvise_collapse` signals to the caller whether the lock has been dropped and reacquired. I initially assumed the `*lock_dropped` flag served this purpose, but that's not what is happening. My initial thought was that if `madvise_collapse` returns `0`, it sets `*lock_dropped = false`. However, as I've now verified, `madvise_collapse` actually clears `*lock_dropped = false` internally *after* reacquiring the lock, and sets it to true only if `collapse_single_pmd` drops it *again*.

The critical issue is that `madvise_collapse` could successfully re-acquire the lock without dropping it again during a subsequent loop. The local `*lock_dropped` remains false, and it returns to `madvise_vma_behavior`. At the end of the loop, the mmap lock is *held* (as required by the caller). This means `madvise_walk_vmas` *believes* that the lock was NEVER dropped and dereferences the original, potentially stale, `vma` pointer via `madv_behavior->vma`. The original VMA, when the lock was dropped and reacquired inside of `madvise_collapse`, could have been freed. Therefore, this leads to a use-after-free when `madvise_walk_vmas` dereferences `vma->vm_end`.

This is a subtle but significant flaw in the logic.  The fact that `madvise_collapse` clears the flag *internally* after re-acquiring the lock is what makes this a problem. If `madvise_collapse` drops the lock *at any point*, it MUST inform the caller.

Let's check the history - `MADV_COLLAPSE` was added in 6.1. The DAMON patch merely calls `MADV_COLLAPSE` - and does not introduce this.  It simply *exposes* the bug. DAMON utilizes `do_madvise` which eventually calls `madvise_walk_vmas` which in turn calls `madvise_vma_behavior` and `madvise_collapse`.

So, it's clear this is a pre-existing bug within `madvise_collapse` itself. The DAMON patch doesn’t *introduce* the problem, but it might *trigger* it more frequently depending on how DAMON uses `MADV_COLLAPSE`.

I did verify that the patch doesn't call `madvise_vma_behavior` directly, it simply calls `do_madvise`, which in turn calls `madvise_walk_vmas`. I also verified that there is no assignment in `madvise_vma_behavior` or `madvise_collapse` to update `madv_behavior->vma`.

Now, if `madvise_collapse` just set `*lock_dropped = true` before returning, this would be okay. Because if `*lock_dropped = true` then `madvise_walk_vmas` would NOT dereference the stale VMA pointer. However, the existing logic doesn't operate this way.

The code currently *assumes* the lock is held when calling `madvise_walk_vmas` from `madvise_vma_behavior`. The comment in the `if` statement correctly says "We dropped the mmap lock, we can't ref the VMA.", implying that it KNOWS about the dropped lock. However, there's another loop in the code! If the lock is dropped, and re-acquired in the NEXT iteration of the loop, the variable is set to false. Since the local variable is set to `false`, the code behaves as if

This is bad. `madvise_collapse` drops the `mmap_read_lock` for each PMD and re-acquires. The problem is that it resets `*lock_dropped` to false after reacquiring the lock, regardless of whether the lock *was* dropped, and even if other threads have modified the VMAs. This is wrong. This is a critical safety concern. Okay, so I'm looking at `collapse_single_pmd` and things seem pretty straightforward. It's doing what I'd expect:
```c
static enum scan_result collapse_single_pmd(unsigned long addr,
		struct vm_area_struct *vma, bool *lock_dropped,
		struct collapse_control *cc)
{
	struct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;
        ...
```
It looks like it's taking a `vma` and getting the `mm_struct` from it. Perfect. Then it gets to this:
```c
	if (result == SCAN_PTE_MAPPED_HUGEPAGE) {
		mmap_read_lock(mm);
		if (collapse_test_exit_or_disable(mm))
```
Ah, right. Locking the `mm` here when it finds a PTE-mapped hugepage is precisely what I'd expect. Now I need to double-check my thinking.

My concern is: does `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp` or `collapse_scan_file` use `current->mm` or `current`? If `collapse_scan_file()` uses `current->mm`, and we're holding the `mm` lock here, that opens up a possible crash if the task switches context and has a *different* `mm`. That's a critical point to verify. Let's dig into `collapse_scan_file` and see if it is vulnerable to a context-switch and thus can access a `mm` that isn't the one we are expecting.

    "thought": "**Analyzing `madvise_collapse` and Privilege Escalation Concerns**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that adds `MADV_COLLAPSE` support via DAMON. My first thought was, "Wait, is `kmalloc_obj(*cc)` a macro?"  I was initially thinking it might be in `scripts/coccinelle/api/kmalloc_objs.cocci`, but that's not right.

I double-checked; the target patch itself doesn't introduce `madvise_collapse` but simply *adds* it as an option. The patch modifies `mm/damon/vaddr.c` to include `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` and map it to the `MADV_COLLAPSE` action. So it's not the patch that introduced `madvise_collapse`, I need to go back and figure out where I saw that macro. Turns out, it's defined inside `mm/khugepaged.c`.

A key concern here is that `MADV_COLLAPSE` potentially has security implications. My thought process went like this: `MADV_COLLAPSE` *should* require certain privileges, or at least ownership of the memory region, right? The man page says it should, but the kernel implementation could be different. I recalled `do_madvise` and how it handles different madvise actions.  I knew that `do_madvise` itself doesn't directly enforce *standard* permissions, the behavior handlers are responsible for doing that. I was especially curious about `madvise_collapse`. Does it check anything about user permissions?

I checked the `madvise_collapse` implementation in `khugepaged.c` and found, no, it doesn't. But, ordinarily, `madvise(MADV_COLLAPSE)` is called from `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(madvise)`, within the context of the user process *owning* the memory. DAMON, however, runs as a kernel thread (`kdamond`), operating on another process's memory.

I re-examined the code. The critical question here is whether the lack of explicit privilege checks in `madvise_collapse` is a bug, because DAMON is operating on another process's memory. Specifically, I looked at how `do_madvise` determines if we have permission.

I know `do_madvise` relies on `can_madvise_modify`, which calls `arch_vma_access_permitted`. I wanted to understand how that function would interact with `MADV_COLLAPSE`, which modifies page tables (collapses to huge pages), but does *not* modify the VMA itself.

I then realized that this might not be an issue for the sealed VMA. The VMA is not modified, so it's irrelevant.

So now, let me double-check `get_lock_mode` to see if `MADV_COLLAPSE` indicates this is a read, write, or discard-style action. It returns `MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK`, which means read, not discard.

In `can_madvise_modify`: it's only concerned about discard actions if the VMA is sealed. Since `MADV_COLLAPSE` is *not* a discard action, `can_madvise_modify` will always return `true` for it, even on sealed VMAs! So this is correct behavior. The key is in that `is_discard` function. I had to verify that this logic holds. I double-checked the callers of `can_madvise_modify` and the code flow in that part of the madvise implementation. So the code is written correctly, I thought.

Finally, I checked whether `do_madvise()` is safe when `current->mm` is NULL, which could be relevant because DAMON runs in kernel context. Specifically, `madvise_collapse` does `struct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;`. So `current->mm` isn't used. And checking the `madvise_lock` calls and the `madvise_walk_vmas` uses a `madv_behavior->mm` rather than `current->mm`.

The last thing I want to double-check is whether `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp` or `collapse_scan_file` might be using `current->mm`, but I *think* those don't.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Safety and Design of DAMON_COLLAPSE**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was whether functions like `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp` and `collapse_scan_file`, and those they call, are safe to be executed from a kernel thread, especially in the context of DAMON. The usage of `current->` immediately stood out as a potential red flag, but the initial review showed it wasn't used, except for `filemap_write_and_wait_range`.  However, `khugepaged`, a kernel thread itself, *does* call these functions, particularly `collapse_single_pmd`.  This is key!  Since `khugepaged`'s design is inherently suited for running within a kernel thread with `current->mm == NULL`, it implies that these functions are, by definition, safe to be called from `kdamond`. So, no problem here.

However, `MADV_COLLAPSE` does have the following:

```c
	if (!cc->is_khugepaged && result == SCAN_PAGE_DIRTY_OR_WRITEBACK &&
	    !triggered_wb && mapping_can_writeback(file->f_mapping)) {
		const loff_t lstart = (loff_t)pgoff << PAGE_SHIFT;
		const loff_t lend = lstart + HPAGE_PMD_SIZE - 1;

		filemap_write_and_wait_range(file->f_mapping, lstart, lend);
		triggered_wb = true;
		goto retry;
	}
```

My next concern was `filemap_write_and_wait_range` since it's a synchronous writeback, but this is a standard page cache writeback and wait, which *should* be safe.

Next question: Is `TVA_FORCED_COLLAPSE` properly respected in `thp_vma_allowable_order`?

```c
	if (thp_disabled_by_hw() || vma_thp_disabled(vma, vm_flags, forced_collapse))
		return 0;
```

It looks like if `forced_collapse` is true, the `vma_thp_disabled` check will return true if `VM_NOHUGEPAGE` or `MMF_DISABLE_THP_COMPLETELY` are set. That's exactly what's expected, ensuring that `DAMON_COLLAPSE` *does* respect `VM_NOHUGEPAGE` and `PR_SET_THP_DISABLE`.  Good.  This means the user's explicit preference is honored.

The commit message mentioning that "collapse action works even when THP is set to never" is clarified. This is because, even though `transparent_hugepage=never` sets a global flag, the `MADV_COLLAPSE` call can effectively override it via `forced_collapse=true`. This makes sense; the user is requesting a specific action, and we're allowing them to override global settings.

Finally, I need to review the changes in the `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` enum value.  Specifically, I need to check to see if there are other hard-coded arrays or `switch` statements that might be missing `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`. I've checked `mm/damon/sysfs-schemes.c`, `damos_sysfs_action_names[]`, and `mm/damon/vaddr.c`, `damon_va_apply_scheme` which appear to be correctly updated. I also need to check `mm/damon/paddr.c`, specifically `damon_pa_apply_scheme`, to make sure it also handles `DAMOS_COLLAPSE`. I will check that now.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Potential for IPI Storms Related to Memory Advise Operations**

Okay, so `madvise_walk_vmas` handles breaking memory regions down by Virtual Memory Areas, and I see that `madvise_collapse` is called for each VMA. I had a quick concern about unaligned ends, but the code aligns the end of the range, so that's not an issue.

Now, I'm thinking about the interaction between `madvise_collapse` and the `process_madvise` path. `do_madvise` uses either the current process's memory map or a remote one retrieved through `process_madvise`.  DAMON uses `damon_get_mm(target)` to get the associated `mm_struct`, using `mmget_not_zero()` to get a reference. Then `do_madvise(mm, ...)` operates on that `mm`.

Here's the problem: `madvise_collapse` calls `lru_add_drain_all()`, which sends IPIs to *all* CPUs. If a malicious user sets up a bunch of DAMON regions with the `DAMOS_COLLAPSE` action, they can trigger a massive IPI storm because each region will lead to a call of `do_madvise` -> `madvise_collapse` -> `lru_add_drain_all()`.

Wait, but `madvise_collapse` is also accessible through the `madvise()` syscall and `process_madvise()`. That means a user could *already* trigger this IPI storm by repeatedly calling `madvise(..., MADV_COLLAPSE)`. So, the IPI storm issue is existing behavior with `MADV_COLLAPSE` itself, not something new to DAMON.  So the question is, is there any rate-limiting on this, or is this accepted as a potential performance impact?

Let me think. Are there any other DAMOS actions that call `do_madvise(mm, ...)` where `do_madvise` takes `current->mm`?

No, wait, I see `do_madvise` takes `mm`. Let's confirm that with the definition: `int do_madvise(struct mm_struct *mm, unsigned long start, size_t len_in, int behavior)`.

I need to check how `damos_madvise` handles `mm` to be certain.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analyzing the Security Implications of `madvise_collapse` with `do_madvise`**

Okay, let me break this down. My initial question was whether using `madvise_collapse` within `do_madvise(mm, ...)` is safe. I need to be sure about potential security vulnerabilities.

My thinking went like this: `madvise_do_behavior` gets called, which calls `madvise_lock`. `madvise_lock` acquires either a read or write lock on the memory map (`mm`). But then I was considering `is_khugepaged` within `madvise_collapse`. The code sets `cc->is_khugepaged = false`, so it seems to behave the same way as `MADV_COLLAPSE` via either `process_madvise` or `madvise`. I was concerned there might be differences in capability checks.

Here's what I've realized about the permission checks:
*   The `madvise()` syscall on the current process's memory map (`current->mm`) requires *no* special capabilities.
*   `process_madvise()` on a remote memory map (`mm`) requires `CAP_SYS_NICE`.

But the key point is how `kdamond` is being used. Since `kdamond` runs in kernel context, it *is* "current", but operates on an arbitrary `mm`. Specifically, `damos_madvise` is a potential entry point, as it calls `do_madvise(mm, ...)`.

Now, digging deeper: `do_madvise` actually handles both `process_madvise` (via `vector_madvise`) and the syscall path.
*   The `madvise` syscall calls `do_madvise(current->mm, ...)`
*   `process_madvise` calls `vector_madvise`, which internally calls `madvise_do_behavior`!

The `do_madvise` function itself: it doesn't do *any* permission checking for `CAP_SYS_NICE` or the `process_madvise_remote_valid`. The syscalls check permissions *before* calling `do_madvise` (for `madvise`) or `vector_madvise` (for `process_madvise`).

So, if `damos_madvise` calls `do_madvise` with a target `mm` (that isn't `current->mm`), does it bypass those checks? Yes, it does. `damos_madvise` operates on behalf of DAMON, which is configured via root privileges (sysfs or debugfs, which are restricted). Setting up DAMON targets usually requires root. So the DAMON configuration itself handles the permission check.

Now, concerning `madvise_collapse`, I need to revisit the locking:
*   The code in `madvise_collapse` has:
    ```c
    for (addr = hstart; addr < hend; addr += HPAGE_PMD_SIZE) {
    	enum scan_result result = SCAN_FAIL;

    	if (*lock_dropped) {
    		cond_resched();
    		mmap_read_lock(mm);
    ```

*   `madvise_collapse` drops and reacquires the mmap lock!

*   `do_madvise` -> `madvise_lock` takes the lock
*   `madvise_do_behavior` loops over VMAs with `madvise_walk_vmas`
    ```c
    error = madvise_walk_vmas(madv_behavior);
    ```

*   `madvise_walk_vmas` loops over VMAs.

    "thought": "**Analysis of MADV_COLLAPSE and DAMON Integration**

Okay, let's break this down systematically. The initial concern was about how `out_maybelock` interacts with `madvise_collapse` and `madvise_walk_vmas`. It seems the crucial point is that `*lock_dropped` signals that the mmap lock was *dropped* and *re-acquired*. This is vital for `madvise_walk_vmas` because it invalidates the VMA pointer.  The code correctly re-acquires the lock and re-looks up the VMA after `madvise_collapse` indicates the lock was dropped. This is good; the logic is sound. This means that `madvise_collapse` is safe to be called from `damos_madvise`.

Next, I considered thread context within the `kdamond` thread. We know `do_madvise` will be called, and it calls `madvise_walk_vmas`, which takes the `mm->mmap_lock` in read mode. `madvise_walk_vmas` itself calls `madvise_lock` which then calls `mmap_read_lock`. For `MADV_COLLAPSE`, `madvise_behavior_valid()` sets `MADVISE_MMAP_READ_LOCK`. Therefore, `do_madvise` acquires the mmap lock for reading, but is the mmap lock held before `do_madvise` is called? The answer is no, because `damon_va_apply_scheme` doesn't hold the mmap lock.  However, `damos_va_stat` and `damos_va_migrate` DO hold `mmap_read_lock(mm)`, but `damos_madvise` doesn't, instead it calls `do_madvise`, which internally acquires it, which is consistent.

Moving on to `MADV_COLLAPSE`'s interaction with `thp_vma_allowable_order`, everything appears normal, and there are no issues. to synchronously collapse memory regions into huge pages by leveraging the

Could exposing MADV_COLLAPSE to automated continuous execution via DAMOS
trigger a pre-existing use-after-free in the core madvise logic?

In madvise_collapse(), the lock_dropped boolean tracks whether the lock is
currently dropped and tells madvise_walk_vmas() if the lock was ever dropped.

If collapse_single_pmd() drops the mmap lock, *lock_dropped is set to true.
On the next loop iteration, madvise_collapse() re-acquires the lock,
validates the VMA, and resets *lock_dropped = false to avoid double-locking.
If a subsequent iteration does not drop the lock, the function returns to
madvise_walk_vmas() with lock_dropped set to false.

Because madvise_walk_vmas() sees false, it assumes the lock was continuously
held and the original VMA pointer is still safe. However, another thread
could have freed the original VMA while the lock was temporarily dropped,
leading to a use-after-free when madvise_walk_vmas() dereferences
vma->vm_end.

While this flaw pre-dates this patch, does introducing DAMOS_COLLAPSE expose
it to continuous background execution by kdamond, making the race condition
much more likely to occur in practice?
